META.subject: Biology
META.subtitleDoc: CBE Phenomenon-Driven Lesson Sequence — Sub-Strand 3.1 (8 Lessons)

UNIT.subject: Biology
UNIT.strand: Strand 3.0: Nutrition, Transport, Gaseous Exchange and Respiration
UNIT.content:
• Types of nutrition in animals (holozoic, parasitic, saprophytic)
• Classes of food nutrients (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, vitamins, minerals, water, dietary fibre)
• Functions and sources of nutrients
• Food tests (Benedict's test, iodine test, biuret test, ethanol emulsion test, grease spot test)
• Human digestive system: organs and their functions
• Mechanical and chemical digestion
• Digestive enzymes and their roles (salivary amylase, pepsin, lipase, trypsin)
• Absorption of digested food in the small intestine (villi and microvilli)
• Assimilation of nutrients
• Egestion and the role of the large intestine
• Malnutrition: deficiency diseases and dietary imbalances
• Balanced diet and dietary requirements across life stages
UNIT.learningOutcomes:
By the end of the sub-strand, the learner should be able to:
a) identify and describe the types of nutrition in animals with reference to local Kenyan examples,
b) classify and explain the functions and sources of nutrients found in common Kenyan foods such as ugali, sukuma wiki, beans, and nyama choma,
c) perform and interpret food tests to identify nutrients present in food samples,
d) describe the structure of the human digestive system and explain the roles of each organ in the digestion, absorption, and assimilation of food,
e) explain the causes, symptoms, and prevention of malnutrition and deficiency diseases relevant to Kenyan communities,
f) appreciate the importance of a balanced diet in maintaining good health and preventing disease.
UNIT.coreCompetencies:
• Communication and Collaboration: Learners discuss food test results and digestive processes in groups, present findings to peers, and engage in structured debates about balanced diets in Kenyan communities.
• Critical Thinking and Problem Solving: Learners analyse food labels of common Kenyan packaged foods, interpret food test results, and propose solutions to malnutrition challenges in rural Kenyan settings.
• Creativity and Imagination: Learners design illustrated models of the human digestive system using locally available materials (e.g., tubing, plastic bags, fabric) and create balanced meal plans using affordable Kenyan foods.
• Digital Literacy: Learners access simulations and videos on digestion processes, conduct research on deficiency diseases affecting Kenyan communities, and use digital tools to record and present experimental findings.
• Self-efficacy: Learners develop confidence by independently performing food tests, recording results, and drawing evidence-based conclusions about nutrient content in foods they eat daily.
UNIT.values:
• Responsibility: Learners take responsibility for their own health by understanding how dietary choices impact bodily functions, applying this to everyday decisions about Kenyan foods consumed at home and school.
• Respect: Learners appreciate diverse dietary practices across Kenyan communities (coastal, highlands, lakeside) without judgment, recognising that varied food cultures can still meet nutritional needs.
• Unity: Learners collaborate in food testing experiments and model-building activities, appreciating each team member's contribution to achieving shared investigative goals.
• Social Justice: Learners reflect on inequalities in food access across urban and rural Kenya and discuss community-level interventions to address malnutrition.
UNIT.sep:
• Asking Questions and Defining Problems: Learners generate investigative questions about why certain Kenyan communities experience high rates of malnutrition despite available food, and why different foods have different nutritional values.
• Planning and Carrying Out Investigations: Learners plan and conduct controlled food tests (Benedict's, iodine, biuret, ethanol emulsion) on Kenyan food samples including maize flour, beans, milk, and cooking oil.
• Analysing and Interpreting Data: Learners record colour changes and results from food tests in structured tables, identify patterns, and draw conclusions about nutrient presence in tested food samples.
• Constructing Explanations: Learners use evidence from experiments and observations to explain how the digestive system processes a meal of ugali and sukuma wiki from ingestion to egestion.
• Developing and Using Models: Learners build and revise cumulative physical or diagrammatic models of the human digestive system, adding detail at each lesson phase to reflect growing understanding.
• Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information: Learners research deficiency diseases using print and digital sources, evaluate the reliability of sources, and communicate findings through structured oral and written presentations.
UNIT.pcis:
• Health Education: Learners study balanced diets, deficiency diseases, and malnutrition, directly linking biological knowledge to personal and community health decisions relevant to Kenyan families.
• Environmental Conservation: Learners consider sustainable food choices and the environmental impact of food production in Kenya, including the importance of locally grown foods such as traditional vegetables (terere, managu, kunde).
• Safety and Security: Learners follow laboratory safety protocols during food testing practicals, handle chemicals carefully, and adhere to hygiene standards when handling food samples.
• Financial Literacy: Learners explore how to plan nutritionally balanced meals using affordable, locally available Kenyan foods, recognising cost-effective ways to meet dietary requirements.
UNIT.careers:
• Nutritionist/Dietitian: Understanding nutrients, balanced diets, and deficiency diseases directly prepares learners for careers advising individuals and communities on healthy eating in Kenya.
• Medical Doctor/Clinical Officer: Knowledge of digestion, absorption, and malnutrition provides foundational understanding for diagnosing and treating gastrointestinal and nutrition-related disorders.
• Food Scientist/Technologist: Food testing skills and knowledge of nutrient composition connect to careers developing and quality-checking Kenyan food products for safety and nutritional value.
• Agricultural Extension Officer: Understanding animal nutrition supports advising farmers in Rift Valley, Kisumu, and other regions on livestock feeding for optimal productivity.
• Public Health Officer: Understanding malnutrition patterns and prevention strategies equips learners for roles in designing and implementing community nutrition programmes across Kenya.
• Biochemist: The study of enzymes, digestion chemistry, and nutrient metabolism provides a foundation for laboratory-based careers investigating biological molecules and metabolic processes.
UNIT.focus: This sub-strand investigates how animals, including humans, obtain and utilise nutrients for energy, growth, and repair, using the everyday Kenyan diet as the primary context for learning. Learners move from classifying nutrients found in familiar foods like ugali, beans, and sukuma wiki, through hands-on food testing practicals, to building a comprehensive understanding of how the human digestive system processes a meal from ingestion to egestion. The unit culminates in applying this knowledge to real-world challenges of malnutrition and dietary planning in Kenyan communities, fostering both scientific understanding and personal health awareness.
UNIT.phenomenon: A Form 1 student from Kericho notices that her younger brother, who eats large quantities of ugali every day but rarely eats beans, eggs, fish, or vegetables, is frequently tired, falls sick often, has stunted growth compared to classmates, and has developed swollen limbs. Meanwhile, her neighbour's child, who eats smaller but more varied meals including githeri, milk, and sukuma wiki, is energetic, healthy, and growing well. Both children appear to eat 'enough food' in terms of quantity, yet their health outcomes are dramatically different. This is puzzling because it challenges the common assumption that eating sufficient amounts of food is all that matters for good health. Students must grapple with why the type and variety of food matters as much as the quantity, and how the body actually processes different foods to extract what it needs — driving the entire unit's investigation into nutrients, digestion, and the consequences of dietary imbalance.
UNIT.supportingPhenomena:
• A Nairobi street food vendor notices that mandazi (fried dough) leaves a grease spot on brown paper but githeri (maize and beans) does not — raising questions about what different foods are made of and how the body treats them differently.
• A farmer in Kisumu observes that calves fed only on water and maize bran without milk or protein supplements grow slowly and develop weak bones, while calves on a mixed diet thrive — prompting questions about specific nutrient roles in growth.
• A long-distance runner from Eldoret (echoing Kenya's famous athletes) eats a carbohydrate-rich meal of ugali the evening before a race but a protein-rich meal of nyama choma and beans during recovery — raising the question of why different nutrients are needed at different times.
• Children in a Turkana County nutrition clinic show visible signs of kwashiorkor (protein deficiency) despite having enough calories, while children in coastal Mombasa show signs of vitamin A deficiency despite eating varied foods — illustrating how specific nutrient deficiencies cause distinct health problems.
UNIT.drivingQuestion:
DRIVING QUESTION: Why do two children who both eat 'enough food' every day end up with very different health outcomes — and what actually happens inside the body when we eat a meal of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans?

KICD KEY INQUIRY QUESTIONS:
1. What nutrients are found in food and why does the body need them?
2. How does the human digestive system process food to make nutrients available to body cells?
UNIT.storyline:
Lesson 1 — Anchoring Phenomenon & Predict Phase: Introduce the phenomenon of the two children (Kericho siblings) and elicit student predictions about why they have different health outcomes. Students share prior knowledge about food and nutrition, generate initial questions for the Driving Question Board, and begin their first predictive models of 'what food does in the body.'

Lesson 2 — Types of Nutrition & Nutrient Classification (Observe Phase): Learners investigate types of animal nutrition (holozoic, parasitic, saprophytic) and classify nutrients (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, vitamins, minerals, water, fibre) found in common Kenyan foods. Learners analyse food composition tables and food labels of packaged Kenyan products to identify nutrient content and functions.

Lesson 3 — Food Testing Practical (Observe & Explain Phase): Learners carry out food tests (Benedict's test on ugali extract and ripe banana, iodine test on maize flour and bread, biuret test on milk and bean extract, ethanol emulsion test on cooking oil and avocado, grease spot test) on Kenyan food samples. Learners record results, interpret colour changes, and construct explanations linking test outcomes back to the anchoring phenomenon.

Lesson 4 — Human Digestive System: Structure & Mechanical Digestion (Observe Phase): Learners study the organs of the human digestive system using diagrams, models, and video resources, tracing the physical journey of a ugali and sukuma wiki meal. Focus is on mechanical digestion processes (chewing, churning) and the roles of key organs (mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver, pancreas).

Lesson 5 — Chemical Digestion & Enzymes (Explain Phase): Learners investigate the role of digestive enzymes (salivary amylase, pepsin, lipase, trypsin) in breaking down specific nutrients through demonstrations and guided analysis. Learners model enzyme action using analogies drawn from Kenyan food processing (e.g., fermentation of uji) and update their digestive system models with enzyme information.

Lesson 6 — Absorption, Assimilation & Egestion (Explain Phase): Learners examine the structure of the villus and explain how digested nutrients are absorbed into the bloodstream in the small intestine. The roles of the large intestine in water reabsorption and egestion are explored, and learners connect absorption back to the phenomenon — explaining why varied nutrients are needed, not just calories.

Lesson 7 — Malnutrition, Deficiency Diseases & Balanced Diet (Application Phase): Learners research and present on deficiency diseases relevant to Kenya (kwashiorkor, marasmus, scurvy, rickets, anaemia, night blindness), linking each to specific missing nutrients. Learners design affordable balanced meal plans using common Kenyan foods and critically evaluate whether the diets of the children in the anchoring phenomenon meet nutritional needs.

Lesson 8 — Model Building, DQB Review & Assessment (Synthesis Phase): Learners complete and present their cumulative models of the human digestive system, integrating all phases from ingestion to egestion with nutrient-specific detail. The class revisits the Driving Question Board to answer questions generated across the unit, and learners write a final evidence-based explanation of the anchoring phenomenon, demonstrating mastery of nutrition in animals.

LESSONS.title: Lesson 1 — Anchoring the Mystery: Why Do Two Children Eating 'Enough Food' Have Such Different Health Outcomes?
LESSONS.aresKeywords: nutrients, malnutrition, balanced diet, dietary deficiency, digestion

slo.purpose: To anchor the unit's investigation by introducing the Kericho siblings phenomenon, eliciting students' prior knowledge and predictions about food and health, and launching the Driving Question Board that will guide inquiry throughout the sub-strand.
slo.knowledge:
• Recognize that eating sufficient quantities of food does not guarantee good health if the diet lacks variety and essential nutrients
• Identify observable symptoms (fatigue, swollen limbs, stunted growth, frequent illness) as possible indicators of nutritional problems
• State that different foods may contain different types of nutrients that the body needs for different functions
slo.skills:
• Construct an initial predictive model showing what they think food does inside the body after a meal of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans
• Generate investigable questions and record them on the Driving Question Board
slo.attitudes:
• Show curiosity and openness by honestly recording predictions even when uncertain, demonstrating intellectual humility
• Appreciate that food choices have real consequences for health, linking learning to personal and community well-being
slo.keyInquiry: What nutrients are found in food and why does the body need them?
slo.purposeInStoryline: This opening lesson anchors the entire unit by presenting the puzzling contrast between the two children in Kericho, surfacing students' prior conceptions about food and health, and generating the questions that the sub-strand will systematically answer — ensuring every subsequent lesson is experienced as an investigation into a real and personally relevant mystery.
slo.safetyNotes: No specific hazards. This lesson is discussion- and drawing-based. Remind students to be sensitive when discussing health and body conditions, as some students may have personal or family experiences with malnutrition.

LESSONS.overview:
This lesson launches Sub-Strand 3.1: Nutrition in Animals by presenting a compelling, Kenya-rooted anchoring phenomenon: two children in Kericho whose dramatically different health outcomes cannot be explained by the quantity of food they eat alone. The phenomenon is designed to create genuine cognitive dissonance — students likely believe that eating 'enough food' is sufficient for good health, and the story of the two children directly challenges this assumption. By surfacing this conflict early, the lesson motivates students to want to investigate what is actually in food, what the body does with it, and why variety matters.

Students begin by hearing the phenomenon narrative and making personal predictions in writing before any instruction occurs. This Predict Phase serves a dual purpose: it activates prior knowledge and it creates a record of initial thinking that students will revise as the unit progresses, making their own intellectual growth visible to them. Students then observe a structured comparison of the two children's diets and symptoms through a guided close-reading activity, extracting evidence patterns that deepen the puzzle rather than resolve it.

The lesson closes with students generating questions for the class Driving Question Board (DQB) and producing their first rough predictive model — a simple drawing showing what they think happens inside the body when a person eats a meal of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans. These models are not graded; they are thinking tools. The teacher photographs or preserves them so students can revise them in later lessons, creating a tangible record of growing understanding across the unit.

framework.learnerExperience: Students listen as the teacher reads aloud the Kericho siblings story (the boy who eats large amounts of ugali daily but is tired, frequently sick, has swollen limbs and stunted growth versus the neighbour's child who eats smaller but varied meals of githeri, milk, and sukuma wiki and is energetic and healthy). Without any instruction, students individually write a 3–5 sentence prediction in their notebooks answering: 'Why do you think these two children have such different health outcomes, even though both eat enough food every day?' Students then share predictions with a partner before three to four students share with the whole class.
framework.teacherMoves: Begin by saying: 'I am going to tell you a story about two children from Kericho. I want you to listen very carefully because there is something puzzling about what happens to them — and your job right now is NOT to look anything up but to tell me what YOU already think is happening.' Read the phenomenon narrative slowly and expressively. After reading, say: 'In your notebook, write your prediction. Do not worry about being right — I want YOUR thinking, not the textbook's thinking. You have 4 minutes of silent writing time — begin now.' [Allow 4 minutes silent individual writing. Circulate and note 2–3 interesting or common prediction types without commenting aloud yet.] After 4 minutes say: 'Now share your prediction with the person sitting next to you. You each have 90 seconds.' [Allow 3 minutes pair discussion.] Then cold-call exactly 4 students using name cards or a random selector — aim for at least one student who mentioned 'types of food,' one who mentioned 'vitamins or protein,' and one who gave a quantity-focused answer. After each student shares, say: 'Thank you — I am writing your idea up here so we can come back to it.' [Record key phrases on the board under the heading 'OUR PREDICTIONS — Day 1.'] After all four share, pause for 12 seconds of WAIT TIME before asking: 'Does anyone want to add something different — something nobody has said yet?' Cold-call 1–2 more students.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Elicit-Before-Instruction (EBI): By requiring written predictions before any teaching, the teacher surfaces the full range of students' prior conceptions — including the common misconception that quantity of food alone determines health. This strategy ensures instruction is responsive to actual student thinking rather than assumed starting points, and it gives students a personal stake in finding out whether their prediction was correct.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher circulates during silent writing and scans notebooks for: (1) students who focus only on quantity (e.g., 'the boy eats too much ugali') — these students hold the target misconception and need the phenomenon to directly challenge them; (2) students who mention types or variety of food — these students have useful prior knowledge that can be leveraged; (3) students who mention specific nutrients by name — these students can serve as peer resources. Teacher notes at least 3 student names and their prediction types to inform cold-calling and grouping in later lessons. No grades assigned.

framework.learnerExperience: Students receive a one-page 'Evidence Card' (prepared by the teacher) containing two columns: a detailed description of each child's daily diet and a list of each child's observable health signs and symptoms. Working in pairs, students read the Evidence Card and complete a structured observation table in their notebooks with three columns: 'What each child eats,' 'What each child's body shows,' and 'What seems different between them.' Students underline or circle the most puzzling detail they notice and share it with their pair partner.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute the Evidence Card and say: 'This card gives you detailed information about what each child actually eats every day and what their bodies look like. You are scientists right now — your job is to OBSERVE the evidence carefully, not to explain it yet. Read both columns carefully, then fill in the three-column table in your notebook.' [Allow 6 minutes for reading and table completion. Circulate and look for students who are copying rather than summarising — prompt them: 'Can you say that in your own words? What is the most important thing this tells us?'] After 6 minutes, say: 'Put a star next to the ONE detail in the evidence that surprises you the most or that you find hardest to explain.' [Allow 1 minute.] Then ask the whole class: 'Who found something that really puzzled them — something you cannot easily explain?' WAIT TIME: 12 seconds. Cold-call 3 students. After each response, say: 'Interesting — so the evidence shows us [restate their observation] but we do not yet know WHY. Let's keep that question open.' Write each puzzling observation on the board under the heading 'EVIDENCE THAT PUZZLES US.' Specifically highlight if a student notices the swollen limbs, as this is a clinically significant symptom (oedema from protein deficiency) that will be explained in a later lesson — say: 'That swollen limbs detail is a really important clue. Let's make sure we keep asking about that one.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured Evidence Observation with Anomaly Identification: Providing a formatted Evidence Card with clear categories trains students to separate observation from explanation — a core science and engineering practice. Asking students to identify the 'most puzzling detail' focuses attention on anomalies (things that don't fit existing understanding), which is the engine of scientific inquiry. This strategy directly mirrors how nutritional scientists and medical researchers identify deficiency diseases.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher looks for: (1) Whether students can distinguish between what is observed (symptoms, diet contents) and what is inferred (cause) — if students write explanations in the 'What each child's body shows' column rather than observations, the teacher intervenes with: 'That is a great explanation — but can you first just describe what you can SEE about this child's body?'; (2) Whether students identify the dietary contrast accurately (variety and types of food, not quantity) — students who focus only on how much food is eaten need redirecting toward the types of food listed. Collect 4–5 randomly selected notebooks at end of lesson to review table quality.

framework.learnerExperience: The teacher facilitates a whole-class sensemaking discussion in which students try to construct a preliminary explanation for the phenomenon using only their prior knowledge and the evidence from the Evidence Card. Students are NOT given correct answers at this stage — the goal is to build a shared explanation that is as good as the class can collectively produce right now, then identify where gaps remain. Students record the class's best current explanation in their notebooks as a 'Working Explanation — Lesson 1' and note at least two things the explanation cannot yet answer.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'We have seen the evidence. Now let us try to explain it — using what we already know. I am not looking for the textbook answer. I want to know: based on what YOU know about food and the human body, why might these two children end up so different?' WAIT TIME: 15 seconds of silence — resist the urge to fill it. Then cold-call 5 students in sequence, using the 'building on' protocol: after the first student speaks, say to the second: 'Can you add to what [name] said, or do you have a different idea?' After all 5 have spoken, say: 'Let me try to summarise what we have built together as a class.' Narrate back the key ideas students raised and write a 2–3 sentence 'Working Explanation' on the board collaboratively, asking: 'Does this capture what we said? What should I change or add?' Once agreed, say: 'Now, this is our BEST explanation right now. But I want us to be honest scientists — what can this explanation NOT yet answer?' WAIT TIME: 10 seconds. Cold-call 3 students. Write their gaps on the board. Then explicitly say: 'These gaps are exactly what our investigations over the next lessons will help us answer. This is how science works — we start with what we know, we notice what we do not know, and we go find out.' Explicitly connect back: 'Remember the boy in Kericho with the swollen limbs and the tiredness — by the end of this unit, you will be able to explain exactly what is happening inside his body and why his neighbour's child is healthy. That is where we are going.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Collaborative Explanation Building with Gap Identification: This strategy, rooted in NGSS Science and Engineering Practice 6 (Constructing Explanations), positions students as co-authors of scientific knowledge rather than passive recipients. By making the class's collective 'Working Explanation' visible on the board and then explicitly identifying what it cannot yet explain, the teacher creates productive intellectual tension — students leave the lesson knowing they do not yet have a complete explanation, which motivates engagement with future lessons.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher listens for: (1) Students who can connect food variety to health outcomes even without knowing specific nutrient names — these students have strong intuitive understanding to build on; (2) Students who conflate 'energy' with all nutritional needs — a common and important prior conception to address in Lesson 2; (3) Whether the class's working explanation mentions anything about what happens INSIDE the body (digestion, absorption) — if not, the teacher notes this as a gap to foreground in Lesson 2. After class, teacher reviews 5 'Working Explanation' notebook entries to assess whether students can distinguish observation from explanation.

framework.learnerExperience: Each student writes two to three questions on sticky notes (or small paper slips) — questions that the phenomenon raises for them personally and that they genuinely want to find out during this unit. Students post their questions on a designated section of the classroom wall or board (the Driving Question Board). The teacher facilitates a brief whole-class clustering activity in which students group similar questions into emerging themes, and the class gives each cluster a theme label.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute sticky notes or paper slips (pre-cut if needed) and say: 'You have seen something puzzling. You have tried to explain it. Now I want to know: what questions does this raise for you? What do YOU want to find out? Write two or three genuine questions — things you actually want to know — one question per slip of paper.' [Allow 3 minutes of quiet individual writing.] Say: 'Now come up and post your questions on our Driving Question Board.' [Allow students to post simultaneously — 2 minutes.] Once all questions are posted, say: 'Let us look at what we have. Who notices some questions that seem to be about the same thing?' Read several questions aloud. With student input, physically move sticky notes into clusters. Suggested clusters to guide toward (without forcing): 'What is actually IN food?', 'What does the body DO with food?', 'What goes wrong when nutrients are missing?', 'How does the body know what it needs?' After clustering, say: 'These clusters are basically our INVESTIGATION PLAN for this unit. Each cluster of questions is something we are going to answer together.' Point to the DQB and say: 'This board stays up for the whole unit. Every lesson, we will come back and mark which questions we have answered — and add new ones as we discover more.' Finally, write the unit Driving Question at the top of the board in large letters: 'Why do two children who both eat enough food every day end up with very different health outcomes — and what actually happens inside the body when we eat a meal of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans?'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Question Clustering on the Driving Question Board: The DQB is a living sensemaking tool that makes student curiosity visible and public throughout the unit. Clustering questions by theme helps students see connections between their individual wonderings and reveals the structure of the sub-strand content organically. Research in science education shows that student-generated questions produce stronger engagement and deeper processing than teacher-provided questions, because students experience the investigation as answering their own questions rather than completing assigned tasks.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reads all posted questions during and after the clustering activity, looking for: (1) Evidence that students are genuinely puzzled by the phenomenon (questions about why symptoms occur, what is in specific foods, how the body uses food) versus superficial or off-topic questions; (2) Whether any students generate questions about internal body processes (digestion, absorption, blood, cells) — these indicate readiness for more mechanistic explanations; (3) Absence of questions about certain important topics (e.g., no questions about what happens to food after swallowing) — the teacher notes these gaps and will proactively address them in later lessons. Teacher photographs the DQB at the end of this lesson to track how it evolves.

framework.learnerExperience: Students individually draw their first predictive model in their notebooks — a simple diagram or sketch showing what they think happens inside the body when a person eats a meal of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans. The model should attempt to show: where the food goes, what the body does with it, and what the body gets from it. Students label their model and write one sentence at the bottom explaining what their model is trying to show. Models are collected by the teacher (or photographed) and returned — they will be revised in every subsequent lesson as understanding grows.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'For the last part of today's lesson, I want you to be a scientist who draws — not a student who writes facts. Draw me a model — a diagram — showing what YOU think happens inside the body when someone eats a plate of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans. Where does it go? What does the body do with it? What does the body get from it? You do not need to know the right answer — draw what YOU currently think. You have 5 minutes.' [Allow 5 minutes of individual drawing. Circulate and make affirming comments about effort and thinking, NOT about accuracy: 'I like how you showed the food moving from one place to another — tell me what happens there in your thinking.' 'Interesting — you drew the food going to the muscles. What made you think of that?'] With 2 minutes remaining, say: 'Write one sentence at the bottom of your model that says: MY MODEL SHOWS...' [After time is up, say:] 'These models are incredibly important. They show me where your thinking is RIGHT NOW — before we have studied anything. I am going to collect them and give them back to you in our last lesson on this topic, and you will be amazed at how much your thinking has changed. That change IS learning.' Collect models or photograph them clearly. Close the lesson by saying: 'Next lesson, we are going to start answering one of your biggest questions from the DQB: what is actually IN the food we eat? We will look at ugali, beans, and sukuma wiki very differently by the time we are done.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Initial Predictive Model Construction: Drawing a model before instruction is a cornerstone of model-based learning (NGSS Science and Engineering Practice 2: Developing and Using Models). It externalises invisible thinking, making implicit assumptions about body processes visible and therefore discussable. Crucially, the model is explicitly framed as a thinking tool to be revised — not a test to be passed — which reduces anxiety and positions science as an iterative, improving process. Comparing Lesson 1 models with Lesson 6 revised models is one of the most powerful learning evidence artefacts in the unit.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reviews collected models after class, specifically looking for: (1) Whether students show any internal body structures (stomach, intestines, blood, organs) or treat the body as a 'black box'; (2) Whether students differentiate between the three foods (ugali, sukuma wiki, beans) in their model or treat them as identical; (3) Whether students show any output or product from eating (energy, growth, heat, waste) — this reveals whether students have a functional understanding of food's role; (4) The sophistication of labelling — models with detailed labels indicate stronger prior knowledge. Sort models into three rough groups (emerging, developing, extending understanding) to inform differentiation planning for Lesson 2. Do not mark or grade models.

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
1. Which student predictions surprised me most — and what does this tell me about the prior knowledge and misconceptions I need to address most urgently in Lessons 2 and 3?
2. Did all students engage with the phenomenon as genuinely puzzling, or did some students already 'know the answer' and disengage? How might I deepen the puzzle for students with strong prior knowledge in the next lesson?
3. Looking at the questions on the Driving Question Board, are there important nutritional or digestive concepts that NO student asked about? How will I ensure these concepts still emerge through the investigation rather than through direct telling?
4. When I looked at students' initial predictive models, how many treated the body as a 'black box' with no internal structures shown? Does my plan for Lesson 2 give these students enough scaffolding to start thinking about internal body processes?
5. Did I successfully maintain the stance of 'not yet giving answers' throughout the lesson — or did I feel pressure to correct misconceptions immediately? What specific language can I use in the next lesson to honour student thinking while gently redirecting inaccurate ideas?

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Students observed the contrasting health outcomes of two children in Kericho — one who eats large quantities of ugali but rarely eats varied foods, and one who eats smaller but varied meals including githeri, milk, and sukuma wiki. Observable differences included: the first child's fatigue, frequent illness, swollen limbs, and stunted growth versus the second child's energy, good health, and normal growth.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Students activated prior knowledge about food and health, and began to understand that the TYPE and VARIETY of food matters — not just the quantity. Students generated questions about what is in different foods, what the body does with food, and what happens when the body does not get what it needs. Students produced their first predictive model of what food does inside the body.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Our current best explanation is that the two children have different health outcomes because their food — even if equal in amount — may contain different things that the body needs. However, we cannot yet explain exactly what those things are, why the boy has swollen limbs, or how the body actually extracts and uses what is in food. These gaps will drive our investigation in the next lessons.

LESSONS.title: Lesson 2 — Types of Nutrition & Nutrient Classification
LESSONS.aresKeywords: holozoic nutrition, nutrient classification, carbohydrates proteins lipids, food composition Kenya, vitamins minerals functions

slo.purpose: Learners investigate types of animal nutrition and classify the seven nutrient groups found in common Kenyan foods, building the evidence base needed to explain why the Kericho children have such different health outcomes despite both eating 'enough food'.
slo.knowledge:
• Distinguish between holozoic, parasitic, and saprophytic nutrition with local examples
• Classify nutrients into seven groups — carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, vitamins, minerals, water, and dietary fibre — and state the primary function of each
• Identify which nutrients are present in a meal of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans using food composition data
slo.skills:
• Analyse food composition tables and packaged food labels to extract and compare nutrient content across Kenyan foods
• Construct a nutrient classification table that links food items to their nutrient groups and body functions
slo.attitudes:
• Appreciate that the variety of food consumed matters as much as the quantity, connecting scientific evidence to everyday dietary choices
• Show curiosity and precision when reading and interpreting data from food labels and composition tables
slo.keyInquiry: What nutrients are found in food and why does the body need them?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lesson 1, learners anchored on the puzzle of the two children from Kericho and began asking why quantity alone does not determine health. Lesson 2 gathers the first critical body of evidence: the types of nutrition and the identity, sources, and functions of each nutrient class. This directly equips learners to name what is missing from the ugali-heavy diet and what the varied diet supplies, moving them from a vague sense that 'something is missing' to a precise, nutrient-level explanation.
slo.safetyNotes: No specific chemical or physical hazards. If real food packaging is handled, ensure no learner has allergies to food residues on packaging. Wash hands before and after handling food samples or labels.

LESSONS.overview:
This lesson sits at the heart of the evidence-gathering sequence. Learners first encounter the three types of animal nutrition — holozoic (as practised by humans and most animals), parasitic (e.g., tapeworms), and saprophytic (e.g., certain fungi and bacteria acting on food) — establishing that the Kericho children and the organisms around them all obtain nutrients differently. The lesson then pivots to the central investigative task: classifying the seven nutrient groups using real Kenyan food composition data and actual or printed food labels from packaged Kenyan products such as Uji Power porridge, Indomie noodles, Mumias sugar packaging, and Daima milk cartons.

Learners work in small groups to analyse food composition tables that include ugali (maize), sukuma wiki (kale), beans, milk, eggs, and fish — the very foods that differentiate the two children in the phenomenon. They record sources, functions, and daily requirement clues for each nutrient class, producing a shared classification table. This tangible, data-driven activity transforms the phenomenon from an anecdote into a scientific puzzle with measurable dimensions.

The lesson closes with a sensemaking discussion in which learners connect their classification evidence back to the driving question: if carbohydrates provide energy but proteins build and repair tissues, and if vitamins and minerals regulate body processes, then a diet dominated by ugali (primarily carbohydrate) but lacking protein, vitamins, and minerals will leave critical body functions unsupported — a finding that directly explains the tiredness, swollen limbs, frequent illness, and stunted growth observed in the younger brother. Learners update their Driving Question Board and revise their initial conceptual models.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown a simple two-column table on the board listing the younger brother's diet (ugali, ugali, ugali, occasional greens) and the neighbour child's diet (githeri, milk, sukuma wiki, occasional eggs). Without any instruction, each learner writes in their notebook: (1) What they think is inside each food — listing any 'ingredients' or 'substances' their body gets from it; and (2) Which specific substance(s) they believe are responsible for the differences in health, tiredness, and growth seen in the two children. Learners share predictions with a shoulder partner for 90 seconds before the teacher takes a cold call.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the two-column diet table and say: 'Before we investigate anything today, I want you to think like a nutritionist. Look at these two diets. In your notebook, write down: first, what substances do you think your body actually extracts from ugali? From beans? From sukuma wiki? Second — write one sentence saying what you think is the key difference between these two diets at a chemical level.' Allow 3 minutes of independent silent writing. After 90 seconds of pair sharing, cold-call 3 learners: one who listed mostly food names rather than substance types, one who mentioned 'proteins' or 'vitamins', and one whose answer is unexpected or creative. For each, ask: 'Interesting — what makes you say that?' WAIT TIME: 12 seconds after each cold-call before prompting. Record 4–5 student prediction words on the board under the heading 'Our Current Ideas' (e.g., 'energy', 'protein', 'vitamins', 'strength', 'growth things'). Do NOT correct errors — tell learners: 'We will test these ideas against real data today.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Predict-Before-Evidence (Elicitation of Prior Knowledge): By asking learners to commit to a written prediction before seeing any data, the teacher surfaces existing mental models — including common misconceptions such as 'ugali gives strength' or 'all food is the same once inside the body.' These predictions become anchors for cognitive conflict when real food composition data is revealed, making new learning more memorable and durable.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans notebooks during pair-share, looking for: (a) whether learners can name any nutrient categories (carbohydrates, proteins) or only name whole foods; (b) whether learners link specific nutrients to specific body functions (growth, energy, immunity); (c) whether anyone correctly identifies a deficiency nutrient for the brother. This informs how much scaffolding to provide during the Observe phase. Target indicator: at least 50% of learners should be able to name at least two nutrient types before instruction begins.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners engage in two sequential evidence-gathering activities. Activity A (12 minutes): In groups of four, learners receive a printed Kenyan Food Composition Reference Table (teacher-prepared, sourced from Kenya National Food Composition Tables) listing ugali, sukuma wiki, beans, whole milk, eggs, fish (tilapia from Lake Victoria), and mandazi. For each food, the table shows values for energy (kcal), carbohydrate (g), protein (g), fat (g), vitamin C (mg), iron (mg), calcium (mg), and dietary fibre (g) per 100g. Groups complete a structured recording sheet: identify which nutrient is highest in each food, and note which foods are poor sources of protein and vitamins. Activity B (8 minutes): Each group receives 2–3 actual or printed food labels from Kenyan packaged products (e.g., Daima UHT milk carton, Uji Power fortified porridge sachet, Indomie instant noodles packet, Jogoo maize flour bag). They locate the nutrition information panel, identify which nutrients are listed, and note any nutrients marked as 0% or absent. Groups record observations on their sheet using the prompt: 'This label tells me that _____ contains mostly _____, but lacks _____.'
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute materials and say: 'You are now going to do exactly what a nutritionist or a public health researcher at the Kenya Medical Research Institute — KEMRI — does when they investigate diet-related disease in a community. Your job is to read the data, not guess.' Circulate during Activity A. At 4 minutes, pause the class and say: 'I need one group to tell me — which food in your table has the HIGHEST protein per 100g? Raise your hand.' Cold-call 1 group spokesperson. Then ask: 'Which food has the lowest protein?' WAIT TIME: 10 seconds. At the start of Activity B, model how to read a nutrition panel on one label: 'Here on the Daima milk carton, I can see it lists Total Carbohydrate as 4.8g per 100ml, and Protein as 3.2g. What I want you to do is find the same thing on your labels AND notice what is NOT listed — what nutrient information is missing.' Circulate and pose probing questions to groups: 'If the brother eats mostly this food, what percentage of his daily protein need do you think he meets?' and 'Does your label show vitamins? Why might that matter for our two children from Kericho?' Do not provide answers — redirect with: 'What does the data say?'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Data-Driven Observation with Structured Recording: Rather than telling learners about nutrients, this strategy positions learners as data analysts using real Kenyan nutrition data. The structured recording sheet prevents overwhelm while ensuring every group notices the critical contrast — ugali is carbohydrate-dominant while beans and sukuma wiki supply protein, iron, and vitamins. Using real Kenyan food labels grounds the science in learners' lived experience, activating the phenomenon connection without the teacher having to narrate it.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks each group's recording sheet during circulation for: (a) accurate reading of the composition table — can they correctly identify the protein-richest food as beans or eggs? (b) correct use of units (g, mg, kcal); (c) on labels — can they identify the nutrition information panel vs. ingredients list? Key misconception to watch for: learners confusing 'energy' with 'protein' as the source of growth. If observed, ask: 'Energy and growth are different things — what in your table is specifically linked to building body tissues?' Target indicator: all groups correctly identify that ugali is predominantly carbohydrate and that beans are the richest protein source in the table.

framework.learnerExperience: The teacher leads a whole-class debrief that builds on the food composition data to formally introduce the classification of nutrients into seven groups. Learners use their data from Activities A and B to anchor each nutrient class to the Kenyan foods they just analysed. They then complete a Nutrient Classification Summary Table in their notebooks with four columns: Nutrient Class | Examples in Kenyan Foods | Main Function in the Body | Present in Brother's Diet? (Yes/Mostly/No). After completing the table, learners write one explanatory sentence connecting the classification to the phenomenon: 'The brother's health problems are likely caused by _____ because _____.' The class also briefly discusses the three types of nutrition — holozoic (human beings obtaining and processing whole food), parasitic (tapeworms obtaining nutrients from the human gut), and saprophytic (moulds on ugali left overnight) — to frame that not all organisms eat the way humans do, though the focus for the unit is holozoic nutrition.
framework.teacherMoves: Begin the debrief by pointing to the predictions on the board: 'Let's see how your predictions hold up.' Ask: 'What nutrient is ugali mostly made of?' Cold-call 2 learners. Affirm: 'Yes — carbohydrates. And what is the PRIMARY function of carbohydrates?' WAIT TIME: 12 seconds. Allow 2 more cold calls. Then work through the classification systematically, asking groups to share data evidence for each nutrient: 'Your table showed beans have the highest protein. Proteins — what do they build and repair?' Continue for lipids ('What foods in your table had fat?'), vitamins ('Sukuma wiki had vitamin C — what do you think happens if vitamin C is missing?'), minerals ('Iron was high in beans — iron in blood does what?'), water, and fibre. For each, say: 'Write this in your Summary Table now.' Use direct instruction only for function — keep it to one sentence per nutrient. For types of nutrition, show a hand-drawn diagram on the board: Human (holozoic) → Tapeworm (parasitic) → Mould on leftover ugali (saprophytic). Ask: 'Which type do OUR two children use?' Cold-call 1 learner. Say: 'Exactly — holozoic nutrition. They ingest, digest, absorb. That process is what the rest of this unit is about.' Close by asking: 'Now write your one explanatory sentence about the brother's health.' After 2 minutes, cold-call 3 learners to share their sentences. For each, ask the class: 'Do you agree? What would you add or change?' WAIT TIME: 10 seconds per response.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Evidence-to-Concept Bridging (Data-Anchored Direct Instruction): Rather than presenting nutrient classification as abstract content, the teacher constantly tethers each nutrient class to the data learners just gathered — 'Your table showed beans have the highest protein.' This bridges the concrete observation (food composition numbers) to the abstract concept (nutrient classification and function), ensuring that classification is not memorised in isolation but understood as the explanatory framework that makes the Kericho phenomenon solvable. The explanatory sentence forces learners to articulate the causal link themselves.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect or scan the Nutrient Classification Summary Tables. Look for: (a) all seven nutrient classes listed with correct Kenyan food examples; (b) the 'Present in Brother's Diet?' column correctly marking protein, most vitamins, and key minerals as absent or low; (c) the explanatory sentence — does it name a specific nutrient class and link it to a specific symptom (e.g., 'The brother lacks protein because he rarely eats beans or eggs, and this causes swollen limbs and stunted growth')? A vague sentence like 'he doesn't eat enough vitamins' without a symptom link indicates the learner has not yet connected nutrient function to health consequence — flag for follow-up in the next lesson.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners revisit the class Driving Question Board (established in Lesson 1) and work in pairs to write two types of sticky-note contributions: (1) A green 'We Now Know' note — one piece of evidence from today's lesson that helps answer the driving question (e.g., 'Ugali is mostly carbohydrate — energy — but the brother needs protein for growth and repair'); and (2) A yellow 'New Question' note — one new question that today's lesson raised that still needs to be answered (e.g., 'How does the body actually get the protein OUT of beans once you eat them?' or 'Why does lack of protein specifically cause swollen limbs?'). Pairs place their notes on the board under the appropriate columns. The class reads 3–4 notes aloud and the teacher groups related questions together, previewing the lesson sequence ahead.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'In Lesson 1 we put our big driving question on the board. Today we have real evidence — food composition data, food labels, nutrient classification. It's time to update what we know and track what we still need to find out.' Distribute two sticky notes per pair (green and yellow, or use different symbols if coloured notes are unavailable). Allow 3 minutes for writing. As pairs come to post notes, ask 2–3 pairs to read their notes aloud. For the 'We Now Know' notes, affirm and connect: 'Exactly — that's direct evidence from your food composition table. That goes toward answering the first key inquiry question: what nutrients are found in food.' For 'New Questions', say: 'That is a fantastic question — write it exactly as you said it. That question is going to be answered in Lesson 3 when we explore what happens inside the body.' Cluster yellow notes by theme (digestion questions, deficiency questions, function questions) and label each cluster. Point to the driving question and say: 'We are building toward answering this. Every lesson, the board fills up. By Lesson 8, you should be able to answer it completely.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board (DQB) as Living Evidence Tracker: The DQB is not a display — it is a cognitive tool. By requiring learners to distinguish between 'what we now know' and 'what we still need to know', it develops metacognitive awareness of the difference between evidence and inference, and between answered and unanswered questions. Grouping new questions by theme also previews the lesson sequence, giving learners a sense of narrative coherence in the investigation.
framework.formativeAssessment: Read the green 'We Now Know' notes as a formative check. Look for: (a) specificity — does the note name a specific nutrient and link it to a specific body function or symptom, or is it generic ('we learned about nutrients')? (b) connection to the phenomenon — does the note reference the two children or the Kericho scenario? Notes that are too generic indicate shallow processing. Read the yellow 'New Question' notes to gauge conceptual readiness for Lessons 3–4 (digestion); if many questions centre on 'how does the body break food down', the class is cognitively primed for the digestion sequence.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners return to the initial conceptual model they drew in Lesson 1 (a sketch of what happens when a person eats a meal). Using a different coloured pen or pencil, they add a nutrient classification layer to their model. Specifically, each learner draws and labels: (a) the meal of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans on the left side of their model; (b) arrows from each food to the nutrient classes it predominantly provides (e.g., ugali → carbohydrates → energy; beans → protein → growth and repair; sukuma wiki → vitamins and minerals → regulation and immunity); and (c) a comparison panel showing the brother's diet (mostly ugali) with missing arrows for protein, vitamins, and minerals. Learners write a one-sentence revision statement at the bottom: 'I changed my model by adding _____ because the evidence showed _____.'
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Take out your Lesson 1 model. You built that before you had any evidence. Now you have data — food composition tables, food labels, and nutrient classification. Use a different colour to show what you now know that you didn't know before. Your model should now show the nutrients inside the foods, not just the foods themselves.' Allow 5 minutes for model revision. Circulate and pose questions to push depth: 'I can see you drew an arrow from beans to protein — great. Now draw what happens when the brother's body DOESN'T get enough protein. What does that look like in your model?' and 'Can you show on your model which of the brother's arrows are missing compared to the neighbour child?' For learners who finish quickly, ask: 'Can you add a section showing what type of nutrition this is — holozoic — and what that means for the sequence of events from eating to nutrient use?' In the final 60 seconds, cold-call 2 learners to describe one change they made to their model and why. WAIT TIME: 10 seconds per response. Close by saying: 'Your model is going to keep growing. In Lesson 3, we're going to add what happens to these nutrients inside the digestive system — how the body actually breaks them down to use them. The arrows on your model today stop at the food. Next lesson, we follow them inside.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision with Evidence Annotation: Requiring learners to use a different colour for additions makes the growth of their model visible — both to the teacher (formative assessment) and to the learner (metacognitive awareness of what was previously unknown). The revision statement ('I changed my model by... because the evidence showed...') is a disciplinary literacy move that ties model changes to specific evidence, practising the scientific practice of developing and using models anchored in data rather than intuition.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect or photograph revised models. Assess against three criteria: (1) Accuracy — are nutrient labels correctly matched to foods (e.g., ugali → carbohydrate, not protein)? (2) Completeness — are all seven nutrient classes represented across the meal, even if only 3–4 are highlighted? (3) Phenomenon connection — does the model show a contrast between the two diets, or missing arrows for the brother's nutritional gaps? Models that show only the food without any nutrient arrows indicate the learner has not yet internalised the nutrient classification as an explanatory tool — these learners need a brief one-on-one conversation at the start of Lesson 3.

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
1. During the Observe Phase, were all groups able to read the food composition table accurately and correctly identify ugali as primarily carbohydrate and beans as the highest-protein food? If not, what scaffolding would make the data more accessible in future iterations of this lesson?
2. In the Explain Phase, when learners wrote their explanatory sentence linking nutrient classification to the phenomenon, how many could name a specific nutrient AND link it to a specific symptom (e.g., protein deficiency → swollen limbs)? What does this tell you about the depth of sensemaking achieved?
3. Did the Driving Question Board additions (green and yellow notes) show that learners are generating genuine questions about digestion and body processes, or are their new questions still primarily about food and diet? What does the nature of their questions reveal about their readiness for Lessons 3–5?
4. How effectively did the three types of nutrition (holozoic, parasitic, saprophytic) connect to the main investigation? Did learners see the relevance, or did it feel disconnected from the phenomenon? How might you embed this more naturally next time?
5. When learners revised their Lesson 1 models, could you observe genuine conceptual growth — new arrows, nutrient labels, comparison panels — or did most models look essentially the same as before? What does this tell you about whether the evidence-gathering activities in this lesson produced genuine understanding versus surface-level activity completion?

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners observed real Kenyan food composition data showing the nutrient content (carbohydrates, protein, fat, vitamins, minerals, fibre, water) of ugali, beans, sukuma wiki, milk, eggs, and fish per 100g. They also read nutrition information panels on Kenyan packaged food labels and noted which nutrients were present or absent. They observed that ugali provides mostly carbohydrates and energy but is low in protein, vitamins, and minerals, while beans and sukuma wiki provide protein, iron, vitamin C, and dietary fibre.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Learners learned that animals can obtain nutrition in three ways — holozoic (humans), parasitic (tapeworms), and saprophytic (moulds) — and that holozoic nutrition involves ingesting and processing whole food. They learned to classify nutrients into seven groups (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, vitamins, minerals, water, dietary fibre), identify the primary function of each group, and locate these nutrients in common Kenyan foods using food composition tables and food labels.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: This evidence directly begins to explain the Kericho phenomenon: the younger brother's diet of mostly ugali supplies predominantly carbohydrates (energy) but is severely deficient in protein (needed for tissue growth and repair), key vitamins (needed for immunity and metabolic regulation), and minerals such as iron and calcium (needed for blood health and bone development). The neighbour child's varied diet of githeri, sukuma wiki, and milk supplies all seven nutrient classes in adequate proportions. The health differences between the two children — stunted growth, swollen limbs, frequent illness, and fatigue in the brother versus energy and healthy development in the neighbour — can now be understood not as a difference in the quantity of food eaten but as a precise deficiency in specific nutrient classes.

LESSONS.title: Lesson 3 — Food Testing Practical: What Is Actually Inside Our Food?
LESSONS.aresKeywords: food tests nutrients Benedict's iodine biuret emulsion test

slo.purpose: Learners carry out chemical food tests on Kenyan food samples to generate direct evidence of which nutrients are present in specific foods, building the evidentiary foundation for explaining why the brother's ugali-heavy diet produces different health outcomes than the neighbour's varied diet.
slo.knowledge:
• Identify the chemical reagents and colour changes associated with testing for starch (iodine), reducing sugars (Benedict's), proteins (biuret), and lipids (ethanol emulsion and grease spot)
• Explain that colour change in a food test indicates the presence of a specific nutrient class
• Link the nutrient composition of specific Kenyan foods (ugali, beans, sukuma wiki, milk, avocado, cooking oil) to their likely health effects
slo.skills:
• Carry out food tests safely and accurately, recording observations in a structured results table
• Interpret experimental colour changes and construct evidence-based explanations connecting test results to the anchoring phenomenon
slo.attitudes:
• Appreciate that scientific evidence from controlled tests is more reliable than assumptions based on food quantity alone
• Show curiosity and honesty in recording results, even when outcomes are unexpected
slo.keyInquiry: What nutrients are found in food and why does the body need them?
slo.purposeInStoryline: This lesson is the critical evidence-gathering session of the unit. After predicting nutrient content in Lessons 1–2, learners now test real Kenyan food samples and generate chemical evidence. Results expose that ugali contains abundant starch but minimal protein and virtually no fat-soluble or water-soluble micronutrients — directly beginning to explain why the brother's diet, though filling, leaves critical nutritional gaps. This evidence will anchor all subsequent lessons on digestion and deficiency diseases.
slo.safetyNotes: Benedict's solution requires heating in a hot water bath — warn learners never to point test tubes at others and to use test tube holders. Iodine solution stains skin and clothing; learners should wear aprons if available. Ethanol is flammable — no open flames when using ethanol. All chemical solutions should be pre-prepared by the teacher. Learners should wash hands after the practical.

LESSONS.overview:
In this lesson, learners move from prediction into direct experimentation — the heart of the evidence-gathering sequence. Working in small groups, they test food extracts prepared from ugali, ripe banana, maize flour, milk, bean extract, cooking oil, and avocado using five standard food tests: Benedict's test for reducing sugars, iodine test for starch, biuret test for proteins, ethanol emulsion test for lipids, and the grease spot test. Each test produces a visible colour change (or lack thereof) that serves as chemical evidence for the presence or absence of a specific nutrient class in that food.

The lesson is carefully structured so that the food samples mirror the diets described in the anchoring phenomenon — ugali (maize), beans, and sukuma wiki represent the brother's and neighbour's foods respectively. When learners discover that ugali extract gives a strongly positive iodine test and Benedict's test but a negative biuret test, they begin to construct direct chemical evidence for why a diet dominated by ugali lacks protein. This is a pivotal cognitive moment: the abstract concept of 'nutrient deficiency' becomes grounded in observable, measurable reality.

By the end of the lesson, learners record a completed results table, write evidence-based explanations, and update both their Driving Question Board and their cumulative nutrient model. Teacher moves are designed to press learners to connect every observation back to the phenomenon, using specific probing questions and structured pair-talk to deepen sensemaking. Formative assessment focuses on the quality of causal language learners use when interpreting results — moving from 'the colour changed' to 'the colour change shows that starch is present, which means ugali is mainly an energy food, not a body-building food.'

framework.learnerExperience: Before touching any equipment, learners receive a Prediction Table listing six food samples: ugali extract, ripe banana juice, maize flour suspension, milk, bean extract, cooking oil, and avocado paste. For each food sample, they predict which nutrients they expect to find (starch, reducing sugar, protein, or lipid) by placing a tick or cross in each column, then write a one-sentence justification for their most confident prediction. Pairs then compare predictions and note any disagreements before the class shares out.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the six food samples on the front bench and say: 'Before we test anything, I want you to think like a nutritionist. Look at these foods — you've seen and eaten most of them. In your Prediction Table, mark which nutrients you think each food contains, and write one reason for your strongest prediction. You have 3 minutes — work silently first.' [Allow 3 minutes silent work.] Then: 'Now turn to your partner and compare your predictions. Where do you disagree? Put a star next to any disagreement.' [Allow 90 seconds.] Cold-call 3 pairs: 'Wanjiku and Odhiambo — what did you disagree about?' After each response, pause 10–12 seconds for wait time before commenting. Then ask the whole class: 'Raise your hand if you predicted that ugali has NO protein.' Acknowledge responses and say: 'Hold that thought — your experiments will either confirm or challenge what you just predicted. Scientists do not trust assumptions — they test them.' Do NOT confirm or deny any prediction yet.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Prediction Commitment Strategy — by requiring learners to commit a written prediction before observing, the teacher creates a cognitive anchor. When results later contradict predictions (e.g., avocado tests positive for lipids, surprising some learners), the contrast between prediction and result drives deeper sensemaking than observation alone. This strategy also surfaces prior misconceptions about 'filling food = complete food' that mirror the phenomenon's core puzzle.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher circulates and reads prediction tables during silent work. Look for: (1) learners who predict protein in ugali — this is the key misconception to track; (2) learners who leave all boxes blank, indicating low prior knowledge about nutrients; (3) quality of written justifications — are learners using nutrient names or only describing food appearance? Note names of learners holding the misconception for targeted questioning during the Explain Phase.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners work in groups of 4–5 at prepared stations. Each group has pre-labelled test tubes, food extracts, reagents (Benedict's solution, iodine solution, biuret reagent, ethanol, water), white tiles, brown paper for grease spot test, a hot water bath or beaker of hot water, and a printed Observation Table. Groups rotate through five tests in a structured sequence: (1) Iodine test — add 3 drops iodine to maize flour suspension and ugali extract; observe colour change on white tile; (2) Benedict's test — add Benedict's solution to ugali extract and ripe banana juice, heat in water bath for 3 minutes; observe colour change; (3) Biuret test — add biuret reagent to milk and bean extract; observe colour change; (4) Ethanol emulsion test — dissolve cooking oil and avocado paste in ethanol separately, pour into water; observe milky emulsion; (5) Grease spot test — rub cooking oil and ugali on brown paper; hold to light after drying. Learners record exact colour changes and whether each test is positive (+) or negative (−) for each food sample.
framework.teacherMoves: Before groups begin, demonstrate each test once at the front, narrating actions clearly: 'Watch carefully — I am adding exactly 3 drops of iodine to the maize suspension. Tell me what you see.' [Pause 10 seconds.] Cold-call 2 learners to describe the colour change. Then say: 'Write down: blue-black colour = starch present. If the colour stays yellow-brown, starch is absent. Every colour change is evidence — write exactly what you see, not what you expected to see.' During the practical, circulate every 3–4 minutes. Use specific probing questions at each group: 'What colour is that? What does that colour tell you is present? How does that connect to the food Kamau's brother eats every day?' If Benedict's test produces an unexpected brick-red precipitate in banana, say: 'Interesting — did anyone predict that? What does this tell us about ripe bananas and sugar?' Use a timer to signal transitions between tests. For safety: 'Before anyone heats a test tube — check that your test tube holder is on and your tube is pointing away from your neighbour.' After all tests: 'Clean up your station, then sit down and complete your Observation Table. You have 4 minutes.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured Inquiry with Anchored Data Collection — the use of a pre-designed Observation Table with columns for 'Food Sample,' 'Reagent Used,' 'Colour Before,' 'Colour After,' and 'Nutrient Present (+/−)' scaffolds learners to move from raw perception (colour change) to scientific inference (nutrient presence). The teacher's repeated prompt — 'What does that colour tell you is PRESENT?' — trains learners to treat colour change as evidence, not just an interesting visual event. Anchoring each observation to the phenomenon (Kamau's brother's diet) keeps the practical purposeful rather than procedural.
framework.formativeAssessment: Circulate and check Observation Tables during the practical. Key indicators: (1) Learners correctly record colour changes using precise language ('brick-red precipitate' not just 'orange'); (2) Learners correctly classify + or − for each food-nutrient combination; (3) Learners who are recording only final results without noting the colour before — prompt: 'What colour was it BEFORE you added the reagent? That matters for comparison.' Target groups that complete tests quickly without recording to slow them down: 'Before you move to the next test, show me your table — is every column filled in?'

framework.learnerExperience: Groups compile a shared class results table on the board (teacher draws the matrix, groups contribute results row by row). Learners then individually write a structured explanation in their notebooks using the stem: 'The food test results show that [food name] contains [nutrient], because [evidence from colour change]. This helps explain the phenomenon because...' After individual writing, groups share explanations in a whole-class discussion. The teacher guides learners to construct a comparative statement: 'Ugali is mainly a source of [starch/energy], but it does not provide enough [protein] for body-building — this is the chemical evidence for why Kamau's brother's diet cannot support healthy growth.' Learners also discuss what is missing from the test results — notably that micronutrients (vitamins and minerals) require different tests not done today, flagging what remains to be investigated.
framework.teacherMoves: After the class results table is complete, say: 'Look at the row for ugali. What patterns do you see? Talk to your partner for 30 seconds.' [30-second pair talk, then cold-call 3 learners.] After responses, wait 12 seconds before responding yourself. Then say: 'Now look at the row for beans. What does beans have that ugali does not?' [Wait 10 seconds.] Cold-call a learner who predicted protein in beans to confirm or revise their prediction: 'Achieng — you predicted beans contain protein. Does the evidence support you? Read me your biuret result.' Then ask the whole class: 'Using your results, who can explain in ONE sentence why Kamau's brother's diet — ugali only — cannot support healthy growth?' [Wait 15 seconds — this is a high-cognitive demand question; honour the silence.] Cold-call 2–3 learners. Listen for causal language: 'because it lacks protein.' If learners say only 'because it has no protein,' press further: 'How do you KNOW it has no protein? What was your biuret result for ugali?' Direct learners to the phenomenon: 'So our food tests have now given us chemical evidence — not just an opinion — that eating only ugali, no matter the quantity, means the body does not receive the protein it needs. Write that down as your explanation sentence.' Finally, ask: 'What did today's tests NOT tell us? What questions do we still have?' Record responses on the DQB.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Claim-Evidence-Reasoning (CER) Writing Frame — by requiring learners to complete the stem 'The food test results show that... because... This helps explain the phenomenon because...', the teacher scaffolds the three components of scientific explanation: a claim (nutrient identified), evidence (colour change observed), and reasoning (connection to the phenomenon). This moves learners beyond describing what happened to explaining what it means — the hallmark of scientific sensemaking. The comparative class results table enables pattern recognition across all food samples simultaneously.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect or photograph 3–4 learner notebooks to check explanation sentences. Look for: (1) Use of evidence (specific colour change cited); (2) Correct nutrient named; (3) Causal connection to the phenomenon present — explanations that say only 'ugali has starch' without connecting to the phenomenon are incomplete; (4) Learners who write 'ugali has no nutrients' — this is an overclaim; redirect: 'Ugali has nutrients — which ones? Which ones are absent?' Use exit data to sort learners into three groups: strong CER writers (can move on), partial CER writers (need sentence starter support in Lesson 4), no CER connection to phenomenon (need targeted reteaching).

framework.learnerExperience: Each learner writes on a sticky note (or piece of torn paper) one statement of evidence they now have from the practical AND one new question that the practical raised. They post evidence statements in the 'What We Now Know' column of the class DQB and questions in the 'What We Still Need to Find Out' column. As a class, they read the new additions and cluster similar questions together. The teacher draws attention to questions about micronutrients and vitamins — which the food tests did not reveal — flagging these as the focus of Lesson 4.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'In your hand you have two pieces of paper. On the first, write one thing you are now SURE of because of today's tests — start with the words: The evidence shows... On the second, write one question today's tests raised but did NOT answer — something we still need to investigate. You have 2 minutes.' [Allow 2 minutes.] Then: 'Come post your evidence statement in the green column and your question in the orange column.' As learners post, scan for the most frequently appearing questions. Once all notes are posted, point to the DQB: 'We now have chemical evidence that different foods contain different nutrients. But look at our question column — several groups are asking about vitamins and minerals. Our food tests today did not test for those. That is exactly what we will investigate next — because those are likely what Kamau's brother is missing most dangerously.' Point to the phenomenon image/text: 'Swollen limbs and frequent illness — could those be related to missing vitamins and minerals rather than just protein? That is our next question.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Progressive DQB Updating — the DQB is not a static display but a living record of growing understanding. By requiring both an evidence statement AND a new question, the teacher ensures the board tracks the productive tension between what is known and what remains unknown. This models how real scientific investigations work — answering one question generates new questions. Clustering similar questions visually shows learners that their curiosity is shared and scientifically productive, building scientific community norms.
framework.formativeAssessment: Read all posted evidence statements before Lesson 4. Look for: (1) Evidence statements that are too vague ('we learned about food') vs. specific ('the biuret test showed that bean extract contains protein but ugali does not'); (2) Questions that reveal strong forward thinking ('why does the brother have swollen limbs if he is eating food?') — these learners are ready for extension; (3) Questions about mechanism ('how does the body use protein?') — these anticipate digestion content in Lessons 5–6 and should be affirmed publicly: 'That is exactly where we are heading.' Keep a photographed record of the DQB state after each lesson.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners retrieve the nutrient diagram/model they began building in Lesson 1 (or a fresh template if Lesson 1 did not include model-building). They add a new layer to their model: for each of the four nutrient classes tested today, they annotate which Kenyan foods they now have chemical EVIDENCE for. They use a different colour pen or a specific symbol (e.g., a microscope icon) to distinguish 'evidence-based' entries from 'predicted' entries made in earlier lessons. At the bottom of their model, they write one sentence: 'Kamau's brother's diet is missing _____ and _____, which are found in _____ and _____ but not in ugali alone.' This sentence serves as the bridge between today's evidence and the phenomenon explanation that will deepen across the unit.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Take out your nutrient model from Lesson 1. We are going to upgrade it with real evidence. Use a green pen or pencil — anything different from what you used before — to add today's tested foods. Write the food name and draw an arrow to the nutrient it tested positive for. This means: you have EVIDENCE, not just a guess.' [Allow 4 minutes for model updating.] Circulate and check that learners are distinguishing evidence-based from predicted entries. Ask targeted questions: 'On your model, show me where cooking oil appears. What nutrient does the evidence show it contains? Now — does Kamau's brother eat much cooking oil or avocado? What might he be missing from lipids?' Close with: 'At the bottom of your model, complete this sentence: Kamau's brother's diet is missing blank and blank, which are found in blank but not in ugali alone. This is your hypothesis — we will keep testing it.' Collect or photograph 4–5 models as formative data before Lesson 4.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Annotation — using a different colour to mark evidence-based additions trains learners to distinguish between inference and evidence, a core epistemic practice in science.
