The model becomes a personal record of the class's growing understanding, with each lesson adding a new evidentiary layer. The closing sentence directly anchors the model to the phenomenon and frames the next lesson's investigation — ensuring the model is a thinking tool, not a copying exercise.
framework.formativeAssessment: Review 4–5 collected models. Assess: (1) Are learners correctly linking tested foods to the right nutrient classes? (2) Are evidence-based entries visually distinguished from predicted entries? (3) Is the closing sentence specific and accurate — does it name the correct missing nutrients and connect them to real foods? Learners who write a vague sentence ('he is missing nutrients') need a targeted prompt in Lesson 4: 'Which specific nutrients? Name them.' Models showing strong annotation and specific closing sentences indicate readiness for Lesson 4's focus on micronutrients and deficiency diseases.

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
1. During the Explain Phase, did learners use causal language ('because the biuret test showed no protein is present') or merely descriptive language ('ugali turned blue-black')? What scaffolding do I need to build CER writing skills in Lesson 4?
2. Which food tests produced the most confusion or unexpected results in my class — and how can I use those as productive entry points for the next lesson rather than treating them as errors to correct?
3. Did learners successfully connect the food test results to the anchoring phenomenon (Kamau's brother's diet), or did the practical become a disconnected exercise in colour changes? What teacher moves could I strengthen to maintain the phenomenon connection throughout the next practical?
4. Looking at the DQB additions today, which learner questions are most scientifically productive and should be explicitly honoured at the start of Lesson 4? Which misconceptions still persist that I need to address?
5. Were all learners physically and cognitively engaged during the group practical — or did one or two learners dominate while others recorded passively? What grouping or role-assignment changes would ensure broader participation in Lesson 4?

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners observed chemical colour changes when food extracts from ugali, ripe banana, maize flour, milk, bean extract, cooking oil, and avocado were tested with iodine solution (starch → blue-black), Benedict's solution (reducing sugars → brick-red precipitate), biuret reagent (proteins → purple), and ethanol emulsion test (lipids → milky white emulsion). Ugali tested strongly positive for starch and negative for protein and fat; bean extract tested positive for protein; cooking oil and avocado tested positive for lipids; ripe banana tested positive for reducing sugars.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Different foods contain different classes of nutrients, detectable through specific chemical reagents. Ugali (maize-based) is primarily a source of starch (carbohydrate) and contains negligible protein and fat. Beans are a source of protein. Cooking oil and avocado are sources of lipids. A diet dominated by ugali alone, regardless of quantity eaten, will be deficient in protein and lipids — and the food tests provide direct chemical evidence for this conclusion.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The food test results provide the first direct chemical evidence explaining why Kamau's brother, despite eating large quantities of ugali daily, is experiencing symptoms of poor health. Ugali provides energy through starch but does not supply the protein needed for body-building and repair, nor the lipids needed for other body functions. The neighbour's child eating githeri (maize AND beans), milk, and sukuma wiki receives a chemically broader range of nutrients. The quantity of food eaten matters less than whether the full range of required nutrient classes is present — a principle now supported by observable laboratory evidence rather than assumption.

LESSONS.title: Human Digestive System: Structure and Mechanical Digestion — Tracing the Journey of a Ugali and Sukuma Wiki Meal
LESSONS.aresKeywords: digestive system, mechanical digestion, ingestion, peristalsis, churning, mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver, pancreas, bolus, chyme

slo.purpose: Learners will be able to identify the organs of the human digestive system, describe the physical journey of food from ingestion to the small intestine, and explain mechanical digestion processes, connecting these structural functions to the anchoring phenomenon of the two children in Kericho.
slo.knowledge: Learners will know: (1) the names, locations, and roles of the major organs of the human digestive system — mouth, salivary glands, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver, pancreas, and rectum; (2) that mechanical digestion involves physical breakdown of food without chemical change, occurring through chewing in the mouth and churning in the stomach; (3) that peristalsis is the wave-like muscular contraction that moves food along the alimentary canal; (4) that the liver produces bile stored in the gall bladder, and the pancreas produces digestive secretions delivered to the small intestine; (5) that mechanical digestion increases surface area of food particles to prepare them for chemical digestion.
slo.skills: Learners will be able to: trace and label the pathway of a ugali and sukuma wiki meal through a diagram of the human digestive system; describe what happens to food physically at each organ; distinguish between mechanical and chemical digestion; annotate a diagram using evidence from the food tests completed in Lesson 3; and collaborate in groups to construct a sequenced physical journey model.
slo.attitudes: Learners will appreciate the complexity and coordination of body systems, develop curiosity about internal body processes, and begin to connect the structure of the digestive system to the real-life health consequences observed in the anchoring phenomenon.
slo.keyInquiry: How does the human digestive system process food to make nutrients available to body cells?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lessons 1 to 3, learners established WHAT is in food and WHY variety matters. Lesson 4 now opens the next investigation: HOW does the body actually deal with food once it is eaten? Learners begin to trace the physical journey of a ugali and sukuma wiki meal through the digestive system, establishing the structural map that Lessons 5 and 6 will fill with chemical and absorption detail. This lesson is the structural foundation — learners cannot understand where enzymes act (Lesson 5) or where absorption occurs (Lesson 6) without first knowing the organs and the sequence of mechanical events. The connection back to the phenomenon is explicit: both children eat food, but the body must first dismantle food physically before nutrients can be extracted — understanding this process reveals why the TYPES of food matter so much.
slo.safety4otes: No hazardous materials are used in this lesson. If physical models or clay are used, remind learners to wash hands before and after. If a video resource is used, ensure volume is appropriate and content is age-suitable. No dissection is involved.

LESSONS.overview: Learners begin by revisiting the evidence from Lesson 3 food tests and making a prediction about what the body must do with food after it is swallowed. Using a large labelled diagram of the human digestive system, a guided physical journey narration of a ugali and sukuma wiki meal, short video or animation support, and a group sequencing activity, learners identify and describe the organs and mechanical processes of digestion. The lesson closes with learners annotating their own digestive system diagrams and updating their Driving Question Board with new structural evidence about why processing food — not just eating it — determines health outcomes.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are asked to recall the food test results from Lesson 3. The teacher displays a summary table on the board showing: ugali contains starch; beans contain protein; cooking oil contains lipid. The teacher then asks: 'You have now proven chemically what is inside a plate of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans. But here is the puzzle — just because starch, protein, and lipid are inside the food does not mean your cells can use them. What do you think the body has to do to food before those nutrients can actually reach your blood and your cells?' Learners write a one-sentence prediction in their notebooks individually first (1 minute), then share with a partner (1 minute), then selected pairs share with the class. The teacher scribes 3 to 4 responses on the board under the heading: 'Our predictions about what the body does to food.' Typical responses expected include: break it down, melt it, digest it, mix it with water, cut it into smaller pieces. The teacher validates all responses and frames the lesson: 'Today we are going to find out how the body begins that process — starting with the physical journey and the physical breakdown of food. We call this mechanical digestion.'
framework.teacherMoves: Display the Lesson 3 summary evidence table prominently. Pose the bridging question slowly and clearly: 'Just because nutrients are IN the food does not mean your cells can USE them — what must happen first?' WAIT TIME: Allow 60 seconds of silent individual thinking before asking learners to write. Do not accept shouted answers during this pause. Use cold-call with equity in mind: 'I would like to hear from someone who has not shared recently — Akinyi, what did you and your partner predict?' After 3 to 4 responses, say: 'All of these ideas are pointing in the same direction — the body must physically and chemically PROCESS food. Today we investigate the physical part. By the end of this lesson, you will be able to trace exactly what happens to a bite of ugali from the moment it enters your mouth.' Write learning target on board: 'I can trace the physical journey of food through the digestive system and describe mechanical digestion.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Bridging from chemical evidence (Lesson 3) to structural function (Lesson 4) using a predict-then-investigate sequence. Activating prior knowledge through the phenomenon connection before introducing new content.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans written predictions to check for prior knowledge and misconceptions. Key misconception to watch for: learners who believe food goes directly to blood without any processing. Note these learners for targeted questioning during the Observe Phase.

framework.learnerExperience: The teacher distributes a blank or partially labelled diagram of the human digestive system to each learner (or learners draw a simplified outline). The teacher narrates a step-by-step physical journey of a bite of ugali and sukuma wiki, pausing at each organ for learners to label and annotate. The narration is as follows — (1) Mouth: 'You pick up a bite of ugali and sukuma wiki and place it in your mouth. Your teeth begin to cut, grind, and crush the food — this physical breakdown is called mastication or chewing. Your tongue mixes the food. Saliva moistens it. The food is shaped into a soft ball called a bolus. Label the mouth and write: chewing forms bolus, increases surface area.' (2) Oesophagus: 'You swallow. The bolus travels down the oesophagus — a muscular tube about 25 cm long. The muscles in the wall contract and relax in waves to push the bolus downward. This wave-like movement is called peristalsis. Label the oesophagus and write: peristalsis moves bolus downward.' (3) Stomach: 'The bolus reaches the stomach — a J-shaped muscular sac. The stomach walls churn and squeeze the food, mixing it with gastric juices. This physical churning continues the mechanical breakdown. After churning, the food becomes a thick liquid called chyme. Label the stomach and write: churning produces chyme.' (4) Small intestine, large intestine, liver, pancreas: 'The chyme moves into the small intestine — a coiled tube about 6 to 7 metres long in an adult. The liver, stored up here, produces a substance called bile. The pancreas lies behind the stomach and produces important secretions. Both send their products into the small intestine. Label all four structures. We will investigate what bile and pancreatic juice do in Lessons 5 and 6.' (5) Large intestine and rectum: 'What is not absorbed continues into the large intestine — about 1.5 metres long — and eventually leaves the body through the rectum and anus as faeces. Label these.' After narration, learners compare their diagrams with a partner and check labelling. If available, a 3 to 4 minute video animation of mechanical digestion (BBC or CK-12) is shown for visual reinforcement.
framework.teacherMoves: Narrate slowly, pausing after each organ. Point to your own body as you narrate: 'I am pointing to where my mouth is — label it. I am running my hand down my throat — this is the oesophagus.' Use a large wall poster or projected diagram for learners to follow. WAIT TIME: After each organ narration, say: 'Label that organ and write the one key process beside it. You have 30 seconds.' Walk the room during the 30-second pause to see what learners are writing. Probe with questions: 'Omondi, what is the difference between what the mouth does and what the stomach does to food? Both are mechanical — but are they the same kind of mechanical action?' WAIT TIME: 15 seconds. Key teacher question: 'Why do you think it matters that chewing breaks food into smaller pieces before it reaches the stomach? Think about what our food tests showed us about what is inside that ugali.' WAIT TIME: 20 seconds, then take 2 responses. Connect explicitly to phenomenon: 'Both children in our Kericho story swallow food. Their digestive systems begin the same physical process. So the physical structure alone cannot explain the difference in health outcomes — but it is the first step we must understand before we can explain what comes next.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Teacher-narrated physical journey with simultaneous diagram annotation — a multi-modal observe strategy. Learners construct their own labelled diagram while processing spoken information, creating a personal reference resource. Organ-by-organ sequencing reinforces the concept of a one-way digestive pathway.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher circulates during the 30-second annotation pauses and checks: (1) Are organs in the correct sequence? (2) Are mechanical processes correctly assigned to organs — chewing to mouth, churning to stomach, peristalsis to oesophagus? (3) Are learners distinguishing mechanical from chemical digestion? Quick verbal check: 'Raise your hand if you can name what the food is called after the stomach churns it.' Target: at least 80 percent of learners respond with 'chyme'.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners work in groups of 4 to 5 to complete a Sequencing and Explanation Task. Each group receives a set of 8 cut-out cards (prepared by the teacher). Each card has either an organ name, a mechanical process, or a food-state descriptor (bolus, chyme, faeces, ingested food). Groups must arrange the cards in the correct sequence of the digestive journey AND match each organ to its mechanical role. Groups then answer two discussion questions in their notebooks: (1) 'In what way does mechanical digestion prepare food for the next step? Use the idea of surface area in your answer.' (2) 'The child in Kericho who eats large amounts of ugali — does his digestive system physically process the ugali he eats? What does this tell us about whether physical digestion alone explains his health problems?' Groups share answers to question 2 with the class. The teacher facilitates a whole-class discussion using the anchor phenomenon to redirect thinking: 'If mechanical digestion works the same in both children, what must be different between them? Where in the story does the difference in health outcomes come from?' Learners are guided to articulate: 'The food being eaten is different — one child has mostly starch, the other has varied nutrients — and even though both bodies process food mechanically, the TYPES of nutrients available after processing are different.' This sets up Lesson 5 on chemical digestion and Lesson 6 on absorption.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute card sets before groups begin. Say: 'Before you arrange the cards, each person in your group should predict the sequence silently for 30 seconds — then discuss together.' WAIT TIME: 30 seconds silent prediction, then group discussion begins. Circulate and listen to group reasoning. Challenge groups that finish early: 'Can you add to one of the organ cards what the food looks like BEFORE entering that organ and AFTER leaving it? For example, before the stomach: bolus. After the stomach: chyme. Do the same for the mouth and large intestine.' For question 2, pose it to the class after groups have written answers: 'Who would like to share their group thinking on question 2 — the child in Kericho?' WAIT TIME: 10 seconds. After one group shares, ask a second group: 'Do you agree, or does your group have a different idea?' Use the responses to deliver the key insight: 'Mechanical digestion is the same physical process in both children. The difference is not HOW the body processes food — it is WHAT is in the food being processed. This is why our food test evidence from Lesson 3 matters so much. We now need to find out what happens after mechanical digestion — that is our next two lessons.' Write on the board: 'Mechanical digestion is not enough to explain the health difference — we need to investigate chemical digestion and absorption next.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Card sequencing as a concrete embodiment of the digestive pathway, followed by phenomenon-linked written discussion to push from structural description to explanatory reasoning. The teacher deliberately leaves the full explanation incomplete to create intellectual need for Lessons 5 and 6.
framework.formativeAssessment: Check group card sequences: common error is placing the liver or pancreas as organs food passes THROUGH rather than accessory organs that DELIVER secretions INTO the small intestine. Address this directly: 'Food does not travel through the liver. What does the liver do instead — where does its product go?' Evaluate written answers to question 2 for evidence of connecting mechanical digestion to the phenomenon. Look for learners who can articulate that mechanical processing alone does not determine health — the nutrient composition of the food matters.

framework.learnerExperience: The teacher directs learners attention to the Driving Question Board that has been maintained since Lesson 1. The teacher reads aloud two or three questions already on the board that are now partially answerable, for example: 'How does the body use food?' or 'What happens to ugali after you eat it?' Learners individually write one new question on a sticky note or card (or in their notebooks if resources are limited) that this lesson has raised for them. Prompts offered: 'What do you still NOT know about how food is processed?' 'What happens after chyme leaves the stomach — and HOW does the body get nutrients into the blood?' 'What does bile actually do?' 'Why is the small intestine so long?' Learners share questions and the teacher adds them to the DQB under a new section labelled: 'Lesson 4 — Structure and Mechanical Digestion.' The teacher also highlights questions from the board that will be answered in Lessons 5 and 6, saying: 'These questions about chemical digestion and absorption are exactly where we are headed. You are building the investigation.'
framework.teacherMoves: Point to the DQB and say: 'Let us check whether we have made progress on any of our earlier questions today. Look at the board — Mwangi, can you find one question from Lesson 1 that you can now partially answer based on what we did today?' WAIT TIME: 20 seconds. After the response, say: 'Partially answered — but not fully. Why not fully?' Guide learners to articulate that mechanical digestion is only the first step. Then say: 'Write one new question this lesson has given you. What does your brain still want to know?' WAIT TIME: 60 seconds of silent writing. Collect questions and sort them visibly into two groups on the DQB: (1) Questions about chemical processes in digestion — 'We will answer these in Lesson 5.' (2) Questions about how nutrients get into the blood — 'Lesson 6 will explain this.' Say: 'Your curiosity is driving our investigation forward. Every question you ask is evidence of scientific thinking.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: DQB update as a metacognitive and community sense-making tool. Categorising new questions publicly shows learners that the investigation is progressing systematically and that today is a structural foundation, not a final answer.
framework.formativeAssessment: Quality of learner-generated questions reveals depth of understanding. Questions like 'What does the small intestine actually do to the food?' or 'How do nutrients get into the blood from the intestine?' show that learners have grasped the limit of today is lesson and are ready to progress. Superficial questions (for example, only restating content from today) indicate learners who need additional support in connecting mechanical digestion to the larger phenomenon.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners return to the personal predictive model they drew in Lesson 1 (what food does in the body) and the nutrient knowledge added in Lessons 2 and 3. They now update this model by adding a labelled pathway of the digestive system showing: the names and sequence of organs; the mechanical process at each key organ (chewing — mouth, peristalsis — oesophagus, churning — stomach); the physical state of food at each stage (ingested food, bolus, chyme); and a note that the liver and pancreas are accessory organs whose roles will be added in Lesson 5. Learners label their model with two explicit connections to the phenomenon: (1) 'Both the child who eats only ugali and the healthy child physically digest their food in the same way — mechanical digestion is the same.' (2) 'But the nutrients available in their food are DIFFERENT — we need to find out how chemical digestion and absorption explain the health difference.' Learners who finish early are challenged to annotate which organs are involved in mechanical digestion versus which are yet-to-be-explained chemical digestion organs, using different coloured pens or symbols. At the end of the lesson, learners briefly share one update they made to their model with their partner.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute Lesson 1 models (if collected) or ask learners to open to their Lesson 1 model page. Say: 'Look at what you drew in Lesson 1 — before we knew anything about nutrients or digestion. Now update that model with the structural evidence from today. Your model should show the physical journey and the organs — but also two explicit links back to our Kericho phenomenon.' WAIT TIME: Allow 4 to 5 minutes of sustained individual model-building without interruption. Circulate silently, noting whose models show sequenced organs correctly, whose models include the phenomenon connection, and whose models are missing key organs (common omission: liver and pancreas). After individual work, ask pairs to share: 'Tell your partner the single most important update you made to your model today and why it matters for explaining the Kericho children.' WAIT TIME: 90 seconds of pair sharing. Close by saying: 'Your model is growing lesson by lesson. Today you added the physical structure of digestion. In Lesson 5, you will add chemical detail — enzymes. In Lesson 6, you will add absorption. By Lesson 8, your model will be complete enough to fully explain why two children eating enough food can have such different health outcomes.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative model revision as the primary synthesis tool of the unit. Each lesson adds a layer to the same model, making learning progression visible and explicit. The phenomenon connection written directly onto the model prevents the structural content from becoming disconnected from the explanatory purpose.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reviews model updates during circulation. Three checkpoints: (1) Is the organ sequence correct? (2) Are mechanical processes labelled at the correct organs? (3) Does the learner include an explicit written connection to the phenomenon — even a partial one? Learners whose models lack the phenomenon connection are asked: 'How does what you drew today help us begin to answer why the child in Kericho is unhealthy? Write one sentence on your model.' This keeps explanation — not just description — as the standard for the model.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following: (1) Could learners correctly sequence all major digestive organs without prompting, or were there persistent gaps — particularly around the accessory roles of liver and pancreas? (2) Did learners make the critical conceptual distinction between mechanical digestion (physical, no chemical change) and chemical digestion (to come in Lesson 5) — or did they conflate the two? (3) Were learners able to connect mechanical digestion to the anchoring phenomenon in a meaningful way — articulating that mechanical processing is the same in both children and therefore does not explain the health difference? If learners could not make this connection, the Explain Phase discussion needs revisiting at the start of Lesson 5. (4) Were the DQB questions generated by learners pointing forward toward chemical digestion and absorption — indicating readiness for Lesson 5 — or backward toward content already covered? (5) Were there equity concerns — learners who struggled with diagram annotation, language of biology, or making the phenomenon link? Identify these learners for differentiated support in Lesson 5.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners observed that both children in the phenomenon physically digest food in the same way through mechanical processes. This means the health difference must lie in what happens chemically to different nutrients — and in what nutrients are actually present in the food — setting up Lesson 5 on enzymes and Lesson 6 on absorption as the next layers of explanation.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Mechanical digestion involves physical breakdown of food — chewing in the mouth produces a bolus, peristalsis moves food along the oesophagus, and churning in the stomach produces chyme. These processes increase surface area and prepare food for chemical digestion. The liver and pancreas are accessory organs that deliver secretions into the small intestine. Mechanical digestion alone does not explain the health difference between the two Kericho children — the nutrient composition of their food is different, and we must now investigate chemical digestion and absorption.

LESSONS.title: Lesson 5 — Chemical Digestion & Enzymes: The Body's Molecular Scissors
LESSONS.aresKeywords: digestive enzymes, salivary amylase, pepsin, chemical digestion, enzyme specificity

slo.purpose: Learners investigate how specific digestive enzymes chemically break down carbohydrates, proteins, and fats into absorbable molecules, explaining why the brother eating only large quantities of ugali still cannot meet all his body's nutritional needs.
slo.knowledge:
• Identify the major digestive enzymes (salivary amylase, pepsin, lipase, trypsin) and the specific substrates each acts upon
• Explain how enzymes function as biological catalysts that break large food molecules into smaller absorbable units
• Describe where in the digestive tract each enzyme acts and the conditions required for optimal enzyme function
slo.skills:
• Analyse demonstration evidence and data tables to draw conclusions about enzyme specificity and action
• Construct and annotate a model of enzyme-substrate interaction using Kenyan food processing analogies
slo.attitudes:
• Appreciate that the body's chemical processes are precise and interdependent, reinforcing the value of dietary variety
• Show curiosity and critical thinking when evaluating why eating 'enough' food in quantity is insufficient without nutritional variety
slo.keyInquiry: How does the human digestive system process food to make nutrients available to body cells?
slo.purposeInStoryline: Having established in Lessons 1–4 what nutrients exist and where they are physically broken down, Lesson 5 zooms into the chemical level — revealing that specific enzymes must chemically dismantle each nutrient type before the body can absorb and use them, explaining why the brother's ugali-heavy diet, lacking protein and micronutrient variety, leaves his cells starved even though his stomach feels full.
slo.safetyNotes: If using iodine solution in the demonstration, avoid contact with eyes and skin; students should not handle iodine directly. No naked flames during starch-iodine testing. Handle glassware carefully.

LESSONS.overview:
This lesson is the chemical digestion cornerstone of the unit's evidence-gathering sequence. Students arrive having mapped the physical journey of food through the digestive tract (Lessons 3–4) and having classified nutrients (Lessons 1–2). Now they must grapple with the molecular question: what actually happens chemically when ugali (starch), beans (protein), and sukuma wiki with cooking oil (fat) enter the body? The lesson uses a teacher-led iodine-starch demonstration simulating salivary amylase action, supported by guided analysis of a data table showing enzyme-substrate specificity, to build understanding that each macronutrient requires its own enzyme 'key' to be unlocked into an absorbable form.

The Kenyan food processing analogy is central to this lesson's sensemaking: just as fermentation of uji (porridge) by microorganisms breaks down maize starch into simpler compounds, digestive enzymes break down food molecules in a similarly specific, step-by-step chemical process. This analogy grounds abstract biochemistry in a context familiar to students from Kericho, Kisumu, Nairobi, and across Kenya. Students will use this analogy to build a labelled enzyme-action model and update their cumulative digestive system diagrams.

By the end of the lesson, students should be able to connect enzyme specificity directly to the phenomenon: the brother's diet is almost entirely starch, which amylase can digest, but his body lacks the protein substrates that pepsin and trypsin would act upon, and his micronutrient intake is so limited that enzyme production itself may be compromised — a powerful insight that advances the driving question toward the consequences of dietary imbalance.

framework.learnerExperience: Students are shown a bowl of cooked ugali and a bowl of cooked beans placed at the front of the class. The teacher poses a written prompt on the board: 'When you swallow a spoonful of ugali and a spoonful of beans, do you think your body uses the same process to break both of them down into small enough pieces to enter your blood — or different processes? Write your prediction and give one reason.' Students write independently in their notebooks for 2 minutes, then share with a partner using a Think-Pair-Share protocol before 3 students share aloud with the class.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the prompt on the board before learners enter if possible. Say: 'Look at these two foods — ugali made from maize, and beans. Both are eaten by the children in our Kericho story. I want you to predict: does your body break these two foods down using the same chemical process or different ones? Write your prediction AND your reason — not just a guess, a reasoned prediction.' Allow 2 minutes of silent writing (WAIT TIME: enforce silence, circulate and note 2–3 interesting predictions to reference later). Then say: 'Now turn to your partner and share your prediction and reason. You have 90 seconds — both partners must speak.' After pair discussion, cold-call 3 students (choose one who predicted 'same process', one who predicted 'different processes', and one who is uncertain): 'Amina, what did you and your partner predict and why?', 'Otieno, your pair had a different idea — share it with us.', 'Wanjiku, you seemed unsure — tell us your best reasoning right now.' Record key prediction words on the board under two columns: SAME PROCESS / DIFFERENT PROCESSES. Say: 'Hold onto your predictions — our evidence today will let you judge which idea fits better.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Anchored Prediction with Reasoned Justification — Students are required to provide a reason alongside their prediction, activating prior knowledge about nutrients (Lessons 1–2) and physical digestion (Lessons 3–4). Recording predictions on the board creates a public commitment that students will revisit, making the cognitive shift more visible when evidence is gathered.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans written predictions during circulation to identify: (1) students who already understand substrate specificity (can advance their thinking), (2) students conflating physical and chemical digestion (key misconception to address), (3) students with no prior framework (may need extra scaffolding in Observe phase). Listen for reasoning quality — students who say 'different, because ugali is a carbohydrate and beans are protein' demonstrate strong prior learning from Lessons 1–2.

framework.learnerExperience: Students observe a teacher-led demonstration testing salivary amylase action on starch. The teacher has two test tubes: Tube A contains diluted starch solution (cooked ugali water); Tube B contains the same starch solution with a small amount of saliva collected by chewing a clean rubber band for 2 minutes. Both tubes were set up 10 minutes before class began. Iodine solution is added to both tubes. Students observe the colour change in Tube A (blue-black: starch present) and Tube B (yellow-brown: starch absent/digested). Students record observations in a structured data table in their notebooks. They then analyse a pre-prepared ARES Evidence Card (or teacher-drawn table on the board) showing four enzymes, their source organs, substrate, product, and optimal pH. Students answer three guided analysis questions in pairs.
framework.teacherMoves: Hold up both test tubes before adding iodine. Say: 'Tube A has ugali starch water. Tube B has the same starch water but I added saliva to it ten minutes ago. Saliva contains an enzyme called salivary amylase. Before I add iodine, what do you predict will happen in each tube? Call it out — what colour does iodine turn in the presence of starch?' (Accept answers: blue-black). Add iodine to Tube A first — hold it up: 'What do you see? Call out the colour.' (Blue-black.) Then add iodine to Tube B — hold it up and wait 5 seconds before speaking (WAIT TIME: 5 seconds of silence to let students process the visual). Say: 'What happened in Tube B? Why is it different?' Cold-call 2 students before explaining: 'The salivary amylase enzyme broke the starch down — so there is no starch left for the iodine to react with. The starch is gone — it has been chemically changed.' Write on the board: SALIVARY AMYLASE + STARCH → MALTOSE (simpler sugar). Then direct students to the enzyme data table (ARES Evidence Card or board). Say: 'You have 4 minutes to study this table with your partner and answer the three questions in your notebook. Question 1: Which enzyme would act on the beans in our Kericho story? Question 2: Where in the body does fat digestion begin? Question 3: Why can salivary amylase NOT digest the protein in beans?' Circulate, target pairs who are struggling with Question 3. After 4 minutes, cold-call 3 different pairs for the three questions.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Demonstration with Structured Observation Table — The iodine-starch demonstration provides concrete, visible evidence of enzyme action that students record systematically. The structured data table then extends this to four enzymes, requiring students to identify patterns (each enzyme has ONE specific substrate) — this is the core concept of enzyme specificity. The three targeted questions are designed to link evidence directly back to the phenomenon (beans = protein = pepsin/trypsin, not amylase).
framework.formativeAssessment: Check that students correctly record both tube results before any explanation is given — this confirms observation accuracy. When circulating during pair analysis, look for: (1) students correctly identifying pepsin/trypsin as the bean-digesting enzymes (demonstrates substrate specificity understanding), (2) students who incorrectly say 'amylase digests everything' (key misconception requiring immediate re-teaching), (3) students who cannot answer Question 3 (may need the lock-and-key analogy introduced early). Use cold-call responses to publicly correct any misconceptions before moving to the Explain phase.

framework.learnerExperience: The teacher introduces the uji fermentation analogy and the lock-and-key model of enzyme action. Students then work in groups of 3–4 to complete an 'Enzyme Action Model' activity: each group receives a card set showing enzyme names, substrates, products, and locations, which they physically match and arrange into a flow diagram on paper. Each group also writes one sentence connecting their assigned enzyme to the phenomenon — specifically, which nutrient in the Kericho brother's diet is NOT being chemically digested because he isn't eating enough of that food type. Groups share their sentences with the class.
framework.teacherMoves: Begin with the analogy: 'How many of you have seen uji being fermented at home? When uji is left to ferment, microorganisms produce enzymes that break down the maize starch into simpler, slightly sour compounds. The enzyme does a very specific job — it only works on starch. Our digestive enzymes work the same way — each one is specific to one type of molecule.' Draw a simple lock-and-key diagram on the board: a large irregular shape labelled 'STARCH MOLECULE (substrate)' and a complementary-shaped enzyme labelled 'SALIVARY AMYLASE (enzyme)'. Say: 'The enzyme's active site is shaped perfectly for its substrate — like a specific key for a specific lock. Pepsin's active site fits PROTEIN molecules, not starch. Lipase fits FAT molecules, not protein. This is called enzyme SPECIFICITY.' Write ENZYME SPECIFICITY on the board. Distribute card sets to groups. Say: 'In your groups, arrange these cards into a flow diagram showing each enzyme, what it breaks down, what it produces, and where in the body it works. You have 5 minutes.' Circulate, ask probing questions: 'Where does pepsin work — and why does it need an acidic environment?' and 'If someone does not eat protein at all, which enzyme will have no substrate to work on?' After group work, say: 'Now write one sentence connecting YOUR enzyme to the brother in Kericho. How does your enzyme explain part of his health problem?' Cold-call one spokesperson from each group (4 groups = 4 shares). Synthesise on the board: 'So — the brother eats mostly starch. Amylase works hard. But pepsin and trypsin have almost NO protein to act on. Lipase has limited fat. His cells are being starved of amino acids and essential fats — not because digestion is broken, but because the raw materials are missing from his diet.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Analogy-Based Conceptual Bridging + Card Sort Modelling — The uji fermentation analogy bridges abstract enzyme biochemistry to a culturally familiar Kenyan process, lowering the cognitive entry point. The physical card sort requires students to actively construct the enzyme-substrate-product-location relationship rather than passively receive it, deepening encoding. The 'one sentence connecting to phenomenon' move forces direct application to the storyline, preventing enzyme knowledge from remaining isolated and abstract.
framework.formativeAssessment: Assess group card sorts by circulating — check that: (1) pepsin is correctly placed in the stomach at acidic pH, (2) salivary amylase is placed in the mouth, (3) lipase is placed in the small intestine with bile noted. The 'one sentence connecting to phenomenon' is the key formative probe — listen for sentences that show causal reasoning (e.g., 'Because the brother eats very little protein, pepsin has no substrate, so his body cannot produce amino acids for growth and repair') rather than just description (e.g., 'Pepsin digests protein'). Students who produce only descriptive sentences need prompting: 'What does that mean FOR the brother in Kericho?'

framework.learnerExperience: Students individually write one sticky note (or notebook entry if sticky notes unavailable) with the heading 'WHAT WE NOW KNOW' and one with 'WHAT WE STILL WONDER'. The 'KNOW' note must reference enzyme specificity and connect to the Kericho phenomenon. The 'WONDER' note should pose a genuine new question raised by today's evidence. Selected notes are added to the class Driving Question Board under the columns: 'Evidence We've Gathered' and 'Questions Still Open'. The teacher reads aloud three posted 'WONDER' questions and asks the class to vote (thumbs up) for which one is most important for the next lesson.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'We've gathered important evidence today about enzymes. Take 2 minutes — write two things on separate pieces of paper or in your notebook: First, something you now KNOW that you didn't know at the start of today — it must connect to the children in Kericho. Second, a question you now WONDER about — something today's evidence raised but didn't fully answer.' Allow 2 minutes of silent writing (WAIT TIME: enforce silence). Collect 'KNOW' notes from 5 students by cold-call and read them aloud before posting: 'Fatuma, read yours.' Post it. 'Does anyone else have a KNOW that sounds similar or different?' Collect 'WONDER' notes from the whole class. Quickly scan and select 3 diverse, high-quality wonder questions. Read them aloud: 'Here are three questions your classmates are still wondering: Number 1 — [read]. Number 2 — [read]. Number 3 — [read]. Thumbs up for the one you think matters most for understanding the Kericho children.' Count thumbs and announce: 'This class most wants to know [winning question]. Let's make sure we answer it before the end of this unit.' Post it prominently on the DQB. Point to the DQB evidence column: 'We now have evidence from five lessons. Look at how our understanding has grown — but look also at how many questions are still open. That is good science.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Know-Wonder DQB Protocol — This strategy makes student understanding visible and public, creating a shared intellectual record of the class's growing knowledge. The voting mechanism gives students agency over the inquiry direction and builds intrinsic motivation. Connecting 'KNOW' notes explicitly to the phenomenon prevents knowledge from becoming inert — students must articulate how enzyme specificity changes their understanding of the Kericho brother's condition.
framework.formativeAssessment: Read all submitted 'KNOW' notes (not just the 5 shared aloud) to assess class-wide understanding. Target indicators: (1) students who can name a specific enzyme and its substrate and connect it to the phenomenon score highly, (2) students who write only 'enzymes break down food' without specificity need re-engagement, (3) 'WONDER' notes that ask about enzyme production, deficiency diseases, or absorption rates indicate readiness for Lessons 6–7. Flag any 'WONDER' note that reveals a fundamental misconception (e.g., 'Does the brother's body have broken enzymes?') for individual follow-up.

framework.learnerExperience: Students retrieve their cumulative digestive system model diagrams begun in Lesson 3 and updated in Lesson 4. Using a different coloured pen or pencil, they add enzyme annotations directly onto the diagram: labelling where each enzyme is secreted, what substrate it acts upon, and what product it produces. They draw small arrows showing the transformation (e.g., STARCH → MALTOSE next to the mouth/salivary gland area). Students also add a brief 'Model Revision Note' at the bottom of their diagram: one sentence stating what they changed today and why the evidence from the demonstration justified that change. In the final 2 minutes, pairs compare their updated models and identify one similarity and one difference in how they represented enzyme action.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Take out your digestive system model from Lessons 3 and 4. Pick up a different colour — this colour will show everything you learned TODAY about chemical digestion and enzymes, so your model shows the history of your learning.' Allow 5 minutes for individual model updating (WAIT TIME: resist the urge to give instructions mid-task; let students work from their notes and the board). Circulate and prompt with specific questions: 'Where on your model is pepsin working? What does your diagram show happening to the ugali starch by the time it reaches the small intestine?' If a student has omitted the pancreas as a source of trypsin and lipase, ask: 'Check your data table — who produces trypsin? Should that organ appear on your model?' With 3 minutes remaining, say: 'Write your Model Revision Note at the bottom: What did you add today and what evidence made you add it?' With 2 minutes remaining: 'Turn to your partner. Point to one thing on your model you're confident about and one thing you're still unsure how to draw.' Cold-call 2 pairs to share their uncertainties — validate and briefly address. Close by pointing to the model: 'Your model now shows physical digestion AND chemical digestion. After our next two lessons on absorption and diet-related diseases, your model will be complete enough to fully explain why the two children in Kericho have such different health outcomes.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision with Evidence Annotation — Using a different colour for each lesson's additions transforms the static diagram into a visible record of growing understanding. The requirement to write a 'Model Revision Note' justifying each change mirrors scientific modelling practice (models are revised when new evidence demands it) and reinforces that the model is an explanation tool, not just a labelling exercise. Pair comparison in the final 2 minutes surfaces remaining confusion efficiently.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect 4–5 models at random at the end of class (or photograph them) to assess: (1) correct placement of all four enzymes at their respective digestive organs, (2) correct substrate→product labelling (e.g., starch→maltose, protein→amino acids, fat→fatty acids + glycerol), (3) quality of the Model Revision Note — does the student explicitly reference the demonstration evidence? Red flag: students who have salivary amylase acting in the stomach, or pepsin acting in the mouth, indicating confusion about conditions (pH) required for enzyme action — this must be addressed in the opening of Lesson 6.

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
1. Did students successfully distinguish between physical digestion (Lessons 3–4) and chemical digestion in today's lesson? What language did students use, and were there persistent confusions between mechanical and enzymatic processes that need addressing in Lesson 6?
2. How effectively did the uji fermentation analogy land with your specific class? Did students from different backgrounds (urban Nairobi vs. rural settings) engage with it differently, and what alternative local analogies could you use if needed?
3. When students wrote their 'one sentence connecting to the phenomenon,' how many demonstrated causal reasoning (enzyme specificity → nutrient unavailability → health consequence) versus surface description? What follow-up questioning strategies would help students who are still at the descriptive level?
4. Looking at the updated cumulative models, are students correctly distinguishing which organ produces each enzyme? Were there common errors (e.g., placing lipase in the stomach, omitting the pancreas) that suggest a gap in the lesson's evidence activities?
5. The DQB voting revealed the class's most pressing remaining question. Does your planned Lesson 6 (absorption) directly address that question? If not, how will you acknowledge it and commit to answering it before the unit ends?

summaryTablePrompt.observed: In the iodine-starch demonstration, Tube B (starch + saliva/amylase) turned yellow-brown while Tube A (starch only) turned blue-black, showing that salivary amylase chemically changed the starch so iodine could no longer detect it. The enzyme data table showed that each enzyme (salivary amylase, pepsin, lipase, trypsin) acts on only one type of substrate at a specific location and pH in the digestive tract.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Chemical digestion uses specific enzymes to break large nutrient molecules into small absorbable units: salivary amylase breaks starch into maltose in the mouth; pepsin breaks protein into peptides in the stomach; lipase breaks fats into fatty acids and glycerol in the small intestine; trypsin further digests proteins in the small intestine. Each enzyme is specific to its substrate — like a key that fits only one lock — and requires particular conditions (temperature and pH) to function.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The brother in Kericho eats large amounts of ugali (starch), which salivary amylase and other carbohydrate-digesting enzymes can process. However, because he rarely eats beans, eggs, or fish, pepsin and trypsin have almost no protein substrate to act upon — meaning his body cannot produce the amino acids needed for growth, repair, and immune function. Even though his digestive enzymes are working correctly, the raw materials are absent from his diet, so chemical digestion cannot solve the problem that physical quantity without nutritional variety creates. This advances our understanding of the driving question: eating 'enough food' by quantity means nothing if the specific substrates for each enzyme are missing.

LESSONS.title: Lesson 6 — Absorption, Assimilation & Egestion: What the Body Does After Digestion
LESSONS.aresKeywords: villus structure nutrient absorption large intestine egestion assimilation

slo.purpose: Learners explain how digested nutrients are absorbed through the villus into the bloodstream, how the large intestine reabsorbs water, and connect these processes to why the variety — not just quantity — of food determines health outcomes in the phenomenon.
slo.knowledge:
• Describe the structure and adaptations of a villus that make it efficient for nutrient absorption in the small intestine
• Explain the roles of the large intestine in water reabsorption and egestion of undigested material
• Distinguish between absorption, assimilation, and egestion as sequential post-digestion processes
slo.skills:
• Annotate a diagram of a villus to explain how each structural feature supports absorption function
• Construct a written explanation linking the absorption of specific nutrients (proteins, carbohydrates, vitamins, minerals) to the health outcomes observed in the phenomenon
slo.attitudes:
• Appreciate that the body's ability to absorb and assimilate a variety of nutrients — not merely the quantity consumed — is fundamental to good health
• Show responsibility in evaluating personal and community dietary choices using scientific understanding of absorption and assimilation
slo.keyInquiry: How does the human digestive system process food to make nutrients available to body cells?
slo.purposeInStoryline: This lesson completes the digestion journey by explaining what happens after nutrients are released — how they cross into the bloodstream via the villus, how water is recovered in the large intestine, and how undigested material is expelled — directly explaining why the brother eating only large quantities of ugali cannot absorb the variety of nutrients his body needs to grow and stay healthy.
slo.safetyNotes: No specific hazards. If physical models or clay are used in model building, ensure no materials are ingested. Remind learners to wash hands after handling any materials.

LESSONS.overview:
This lesson is the sixth in the evidence-gathering sequence for Sub-Strand 3.1 and represents the Explain Phase of the unit storyline. Having investigated mechanical digestion, chemical digestion, and the enzymes acting on ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans in previous lessons, learners now follow the digested products to their final destinations: absorption across the wall of the small intestine, assimilation into body cells, water reabsorption in the large intestine, and egestion of waste. The central sensemaking tool is the villus — its finger-like shape, thin epithelium, rich blood capillary network, and lacteal — which learners annotate and explain as a structure precisely adapted for efficient absorption.

The lesson uses a structured analysis of a villus diagram alongside a guided reading passage describing what happens to the glucose, amino acids, fatty acids, vitamins, and minerals released from a meal of githeri and sukuma wiki. Learners explicitly connect each absorbed nutrient type to a body function and then re-examine the phenomenon: why does the brother who eats large quantities of ugali but almost no beans, eggs, or vegetables fail to absorb sufficient proteins, iron, vitamins, and minerals? The answer lies not only in what was eaten but in what could be absorbed — and what was never present to be absorbed in the first place.

By the end of the lesson, learners update their Driving Question Board with new evidence and revise their cumulative body-process model to include arrows showing absorption routes through the villus wall into blood capillaries and lacteals, transport to the liver, and eventual assimilation into body tissues. This positions learners to address dietary deficiency diseases and transport in the next lessons.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown a simple outline diagram of the small intestine with the question: 'A meal of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans has been fully digested — the large molecules have been broken down into glucose, amino acids, fatty acids, vitamins, and minerals. Where do these small molecules go next, and how do they get there?' Students write a 3-sentence individual prediction in their notebooks, drawing a quick sketch if they wish. They then share their prediction with a partner using a Think-Pair-Share protocol before two or three pairs are cold-called to share with the class.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the outline diagram of the small intestine on the board and say: 'In our last lessons we tracked what enzymes do to ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans — breaking starch into glucose, proteins into amino acids, and fats into fatty acids and glycerol. Today we ask: once these tiny molecules are floating in the small intestine, what actually happens to them? Take 2 minutes — silently — write your prediction and draw a quick sketch.' [WAIT TIME: 2 minutes silent writing.] 'Now share with your partner — you have 90 seconds each.' [WAIT TIME: 3 minutes pair discussion.] Cold-call 3 pairs: 'Amina and Brian — what did you predict happens to glucose?' [WAIT 10 seconds.] 'Fatuma and Otieno — did your prediction agree or differ?' [WAIT 10 seconds.] 'Wanjiku and Kipchoge — what about the vitamins and minerals from the sukuma wiki?' [WAIT 12 seconds.] Record key prediction words on the board (e.g., 'into blood,' 'through the wall,' 'absorbed') — do not correct yet. Say: 'Hold these predictions — we are about to gather evidence to test them.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Think-Pair-Share with Anchored Prediction: Students activate prior knowledge of digestion products from previous lessons and make testable predictions about absorption. Recording predictions publicly on the board creates accountability and a reference point for revision during the Explain Phase, building metacognitive awareness of how understanding evolves.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher listens for whether students can name the end-products of digestion (glucose, amino acids, fatty acids) from prior lessons — this reveals retention of Lessons 3–5 content. Watch for students who predict only 'blood absorption' without any mechanism — these students need scaffolding during the Observe Phase. Note any student who mentions the intestinal wall or surface area — this indicates readiness for villus detail.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners receive a two-part resource: (1) a detailed labelled diagram of a single villus showing the epithelial cell layer, blood capillaries, lacteal, microvilli (brush border), thin wall, and rich blood supply; and (2) a structured guided reading passage (approximately 250 words) describing how glucose and amino acids cross the thin epithelium into blood capillaries, how fatty acids and glycerol enter the lacteal (lymph vessel), and how vitamins and minerals are absorbed alongside these routes. Learners complete an observation table with four columns: Nutrient Type | Absorption Route | Structure Used | Why This Structure Helps. They work individually for 6 minutes, then compare tables with a partner.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute the villus diagram and guided reading. Say: 'You have 6 minutes to read carefully and complete the observation table. Focus on two things: what structure does each nutrient use, and why is that structure suited for the job?' [WAIT TIME: 6 minutes individual work — circulate, do not explain yet, only prompt with: 'What does the reading say about fatty acids — find that sentence.'] After 6 minutes: 'Compare your table with your partner. Do you agree on the absorption route for glucose? For fatty acids?' [2 minutes pair comparison.] Bring class together. Cold-call 4 students: 'Kamau — which route does glucose take and why?' [WAIT 10 seconds.] 'Akinyi — what is the lacteal and what enters it?' [WAIT 12 seconds.] 'Mwangi — look at the diagram — give me TWO features of the villus that increase the speed of absorption.' [WAIT 15 seconds — this is a higher-order observation.] 'Chebet — the microvilli are called the brush border — what do you think they do to the surface area?' [WAIT 12 seconds.] Draw attention to the thin epithelium: 'This wall is only ONE cell thick — what advantage does that give for absorption speed? Think about our brother in Kericho who is only eating ugali. He has a working villus — but what is missing from his intestine to be absorbed?' [WAIT 15 seconds — do not answer this yet; this bridges to Explain Phase.]
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured Observation Table with Diagram Annotation: The four-column table scaffolds systematic observation by requiring learners to move from identifying a fact (nutrient type) to explaining a mechanism (why the structure helps). The diagram anchors abstract text to a visual structure, supporting both verbal and visual learners. The bridge question at the end of teacher moves connects the observation directly back to the phenomenon before the Explain Phase begins.
framework.formativeAssessment: Check observation tables during circulation — look for: (1) correct pairing of glucose/amino acids → blood capillaries and fatty acids/glycerol → lacteal; (2) at least two structural adaptations named (thin wall, large surface area, rich blood supply, microvilli). A common misconception to watch for is students writing 'fats go into blood directly' — address this during cold-call. Students who cannot locate information in the reading may need the teacher to point to specific paragraphs.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners engage in a three-part sensemaking sequence. First, they annotate their own blank villus outline (provided) by drawing arrows showing absorption routes and labelling structural adaptations in their own words. Second, they read a short paragraph on assimilation — how absorbed nutrients are used by body cells (glucose for energy via respiration, amino acids for building muscle and enzymes, fatty acids for cell membranes, iron for haemoglobin, calcium for bones) — and match each nutrient to a body function using a matching activity. Third, the teacher leads a whole-class discussion linking all processes back to the phenomenon: 'We now know how nutrients are absorbed — so let us go back to Kericho. Why is the brother who eats large amounts of ugali still tired, sick, stunted, and swollen?'
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Now you are going to be the biology teacher — annotate your blank villus diagram. Write your own labels in your own words. You have 4 minutes.' [WAIT TIME: 4 minutes — circulate and check for arrows showing directionality, thin wall labelled, capillary and lacteal distinguished.] 'Now complete the nutrient-to-function matching activity — 3 minutes.' [WAIT TIME: 3 minutes.] Bring class together for the phenomenon discussion. Write on board: 'The brother eats ugali all day. His villus is working perfectly. So why is he sick?' Cold-call: 'Atieno — his villus can absorb glucose from ugali. What does glucose give the body?' [WAIT 10 seconds.] 'Omondi — if the brother is not eating beans, eggs, or fish — what amino acids are missing from his small intestine?' [WAIT 12 seconds.] 'Wambui — he has swollen limbs. We learned earlier this can be caused by protein deficiency. Connect that to absorption — if no protein enters the small intestine, what does the villus absorb?' [WAIT 15 seconds.] 'Ngugi — sukuma wiki provides iron and vitamins. His neighbour's child eats sukuma wiki daily. What can that child's blood do that the brother's cannot?' [WAIT 12 seconds.] Summarise: 'The villus is not the problem. The problem is what is presented to the villus. A diet with only ugali presents only glucose. The body cannot make haemoglobin, build muscle, or fight infection from glucose alone. Quantity of food without variety means the villus absorbs a very limited set of nutrients — and the body suffers for everything that is missing.' Write this as a class 'Explain Statement' on the board for students to copy.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Annotate-then-Connect (Diagram Annotation + Phenomenon Return): Annotation forces learners to re-encode structural information in their own words and spatial format, deepening encoding. The phenomenon return discussion uses Socratic cold-calling to build a causal chain: food variety → presence of diverse nutrients in small intestine → absorption of diverse nutrients through villus → assimilation into diverse body functions → health. This chain directly answers the driving question and makes visible the logic that 'enough food in quantity' is insufficient without 'enough food in variety.'
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect annotated villus diagrams at the end of the phase — look for: (1) arrows showing glucose/amino acids entering capillary and fatty acids entering lacteal; (2) at least three structural adaptations labelled with functional explanations; (3) evidence of understanding that the villus absorbs only what is present. During discussion, listen for students who can articulate the causal chain (missing food type → missing nutrient in intestine → nothing to absorb → deficiency in body). Students who respond with only 'he doesn't eat enough' are still operating on a quantity-only framework and need additional probing.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners revisit the class Driving Question Board which has accumulated evidence cards from Lessons 1–5. Each student writes two new sticky note contributions: one 'Evidence Card' (green) stating what new evidence this lesson added — e.g., 'Glucose and amino acids are absorbed through the thin epithelium of the villus into blood capillaries' — and one 'Connection Card' (yellow) linking this evidence to either the driving question or the phenomenon — e.g., 'This explains why the brother's body cannot build muscle — no amino acids from beans means nothing for the villus to absorb into blood.' Learners also update the section of the DQB labelled 'What we still need to explain' by noting: 'What happens to absorbed nutrients in the blood? Where do they go after the villus?'
framework.teacherMoves: Direct students to the DQB: 'Look at the board. In Lesson 1 we recorded our first questions about the two children. In Lessons 3, 4, and 5 we added evidence about digestion. Today we complete the digestion-to-absorption story.' Distribute green and yellow sticky notes. Say: 'On your green card, write ONE specific piece of evidence from today's lesson — a fact about absorption or the villus. On your yellow card, write how that evidence helps explain the Kericho phenomenon. You have 3 minutes.' [WAIT TIME: 3 minutes writing.] Ask 4 students to read their cards aloud before posting: 'Njeri — read your green card.' [WAIT 8 seconds.] 'Abdi — read your yellow card.' [WAIT 8 seconds.] 'Amina — does your yellow card agree with Abdi's or take a different angle?' [WAIT 10 seconds.] 'Simiyu — what did you write under 'What we still need to explain'?' [WAIT 10 seconds.] Post all cards and step back: 'Look at the board. We have now explained what happens from the moment ugali enters the mouth to the moment glucose enters the blood. But absorbed nutrients are now in the blood — where do they go? That will lead us into our next sub-strand: Transport. The DQB is telling us the story of our own investigation.' Point explicitly to any unanswered questions from Lesson 1 that can now be crossed off and marked 'EXPLAINED.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Dual-Card DQB Update (Evidence + Connection): Requiring two separate card types — one factual, one connective — prevents students from simply restating content without making meaning. The Connection Card specifically demands that learners perform the transfer from biological mechanism to real-world health outcome, which is the core competency of the unit. Publicly posting and reading cards creates a shared knowledge artefact that the whole class can reference, and crossing off explained questions makes learning progress visible and motivating.
framework.formativeAssessment: Read DQB cards as they are posted — look for Connection Cards that go beyond surface statements ('it explains absorption') to causal reasoning ('because the brother never eats beans, the villus has no amino acids to absorb, so his muscles and immune cells cannot be built'). Count how many students can name a specific nutrient and link it to a specific deficiency symptom from the phenomenon — this is the target understanding for the lesson. Students writing only general statements need a follow-up probe question during the next lesson's opening.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners retrieve their cumulative body-process model begun in Lesson 1 (a diagram tracking the journey of a meal of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans through the digestive system). They now add a detailed absorption section: drawing a villus in the small intestine wall, labelling the epithelium, blood capillaries, and lacteal, and drawing colour-coded arrows showing glucose and amino acids entering the capillary (red arrows) and fatty acids entering the lacteal (blue arrows). They also add a large intestine section showing water reabsorption with an arrow indicating 'water → blood' and a terminal section labelled 'egestion of undigested material (faeces).' Finally, they add a small box labelled 'Assimilation' with three examples: glucose → energy for Chebet to run; amino acids → muscle repair for Kipchoge's training; iron (from sukuma wiki) → haemoglobin for carrying oxygen. Students write a one-sentence 'Model Claim' at the bottom: 'The body can only assimilate nutrients that were present in food, digested, and successfully absorbed — variety in diet determines variety in absorption.'
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Get out your cumulative model. You have been building this since Lesson 1. Today we add the final chapter of digestion: absorption, assimilation, and egestion. You have 5 minutes to add the villus, label the absorption routes, add the large intestine, and write your Model Claim.' [WAIT TIME: 5 minutes — circulate actively, checking that students: (1) distinguish capillary from lacteal routes; (2) include the large intestine and water reabsorption; (3) include egestion; (4) write a Model Claim that references variety, not just process.] With 2 minutes remaining, cold-call two students to share their Model Claim aloud: 'Muthoni — read your Model Claim.' [WAIT 8 seconds.] 'Does anyone have a Model Claim that adds something Muthoni's did not mention? Baraka?' [WAIT 10 seconds.] Close by saying: 'Hold onto this model. In Lesson 7 we will trace what happens to nutrients once they are in the blood — the transport system picks up exactly where absorption ends. Your model will grow again.' If time permits (2 minutes), ask: 'Turn to a partner — in one sentence, explain to them why the Kericho brother cannot solve his health problem simply by eating more ugali, using the word villus in your answer.' [WAIT 90 seconds.] Cold-call one final student: 'Wafula — what did you tell your partner?' [WAIT 10 seconds.]
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision with Model Claim: The cumulative model serves as a running scientific explanation artefact — each lesson's additions show learners that understanding builds incrementally, mirroring how scientists develop models over time. The Model Claim requirement forces consolidation of the lesson's core idea into a single transferable statement, which also serves as a self-assessment tool. The final partner explanation using the target word 'villus' checks for conceptual fluency rather than memorisation.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect or photograph cumulative models at the end of the lesson — look for: (1) villus drawn in small intestine wall with at least two correctly labelled structures; (2) two distinct colour-coded absorption routes (capillary vs. lacteal); (3) large intestine with water reabsorption arrow; (4) egestion labelled at the terminal end; (5) a Model Claim that explicitly names 'variety' or names specific nutrients rather than only describing the absorption process generically. Models missing the large intestine section indicate the egestion content was not encoded — teacher should address this at the start of Lesson 7.

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
1. During the Explain Phase discussion, were students able to construct a causal chain from 'missing food type' to 'missing nutrient in intestine' to 'nothing for villus to absorb' to 'specific health deficit in the phenomenon'? Which students could do this independently and which required scaffolding through cold-call prompts?
2. Did learners consistently distinguish between absorption (nutrient crossing intestinal wall into blood or lymph) and assimilation (nutrient being used by body cells), or did these two terms remain conflated? What additional strategy could clarify this distinction in Lesson 7?
3. When students annotated their villus diagrams, how accurately did they explain WHY each structural feature supports absorption function — not just naming the feature? Did the observation table scaffold this sufficiently or does the task design need revision?
4. Looking at the DQB Connection Cards posted today, how many students moved beyond surface statements to specific causal reasoning linking a named nutrient to a named deficiency symptom from the Kericho phenomenon? What does this tell me about readiness for Lesson 7 on transport?
5. Was 40 minutes sufficient for all five phases, particularly the Model Building Phase? Which phase required more time than planned, and how would I restructure the timing for the next cohort while preserving the depth of sensemaking in the Explain Phase?

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Students observed the structure of a villus — its thin epithelium, blood capillaries, lacteal, and microvilli — and tracked the specific absorption routes for glucose and amino acids (into blood capillaries), fatty acids and glycerol (into lacteal), vitamins, and minerals. They also observed the role of the large intestine in water reabsorption and egestion of undigested material.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Digested nutrients are absorbed through the thin wall of the villus in the small intestine into the bloodstream or lymphatic system; water is reabsorbed in the large intestine and undigested material is expelled as faeces; assimilation is the use of absorbed nutrients by body cells for specific functions including energy production, growth, and immune defence; the villus can only absorb what is physically present — it cannot compensate for nutrients missing from the diet.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: This explains why the Kericho brother who eats large quantities of ugali but almost no beans, vegetables, or animal products remains sick, tired, stunted, and swollen despite eating 'enough' food: his working villus can only absorb the glucose produced from digesting ugali starch. Because he eats virtually no proteins, iron-rich foods, or vitamins, there are no amino acids, iron, or fat-soluble vitamins present in his small intestine for the villus to absorb — no matter how efficiently his digestive system works. His neighbour's child, eating varied githeri, sukuma wiki, and milk, provides the villus with a full range of nutrients to absorb and assimilate, supporting energy, immunity, growth, and oxygen transport — producing dramatically better health outcomes from smaller but more varied meals.

LESSONS.title: Lesson 7 — Malnutrition, Deficiency Diseases & Designing a Balanced Diet
LESSONS.aresKeywords: deficiency diseases Kenya, kwashiorkor marasmus, balanced diet nutrients, malnutrition Africa, dietary planning

slo.purpose: Learners synthesise all prior knowledge about nutrients and digestion to explain why the brother in the phenomenon is sick, identify specific deficiency diseases caused by missing nutrients, and design affordable balanced meal plans using common Kenyan foods.
slo.knowledge:
• Identify and describe at least six deficiency diseases (kwashiorkor, marasmus, scurvy, rickets, anaemia, night blindness) and their causative nutritional deficiencies
• Explain the health consequences of eating a high-quantity but nutritionally imbalanced diet, as in the phenomenon
• Describe the characteristics of a balanced diet in terms of proportion and variety of nutrients required by the body
slo.skills:
• Analyse and evaluate the nutritional adequacy of diets using evidence gathered across the lesson sequence
• Design an affordable, balanced meal plan using locally available Kenyan foods that addresses identified deficiencies
slo.attitudes:
• Appreciate that food quality and variety are as critical as quantity in maintaining health and preventing disease
• Show responsibility and empathy in considering food access challenges faced by Kenyan families when designing meal plans
slo.keyInquiry: What nutrients are found in food and why does the body need them? (Applied to consequences of absence)
slo.purposeInStoryline: This lesson is the application and synthesis phase of the entire unit — learners now use all evidence gathered about nutrients, digestion, and absorption (Lessons 1–6) to fully explain the phenomenon, name the diseases the brother likely has, and propose practical, affordable dietary solutions grounded in Kenyan food culture.
slo.safetyNotes: No specific hazards. Ensure sensitivity when discussing malnutrition — some students may have personal or family experiences with food insecurity. Remind learners that this is a scientific investigation context, not a platform for judging families.

LESSONS.overview:
In this penultimate lesson of the evidence-gathering sequence, learners arrive having built substantial knowledge about macronutrients, micronutrients, digestion, and absorption across Lessons 1–6. The central focus now shifts to consequences: what happens when specific nutrients are chronically absent from the diet? Learners examine six key deficiency diseases prevalent in Kenya — kwashiorkor, marasmus, scurvy, rickets, anaemia, and night blindness — linking each to the specific nutrient(s) missing, the body systems affected, and the visible symptoms. This directly illuminates the phenomenon: the brother eating large quantities of ugali but lacking protein, vitamins, and minerals is not simply 'malnourished in quantity' but is suffering from multiple specific, nameable, and preventable nutritional deficiencies.

The lesson uses a jigsaw research-and-present structure in which small groups become 'experts' on one deficiency disease and teach the rest of the class, building a shared class reference table. This collaborative strategy is explicitly KICD-aligned and mirrors the evidence-gathering approach of the storyline. Learners then apply this knowledge by critically evaluating the two diets in the phenomenon and designing their own affordable, balanced meal plans using foods available in Kenyan markets — githeri, milk, green vegetables, eggs, fish, beans, sweet potatoes, and fruits.

By the end of this lesson, learners can fully answer both key inquiry questions and have strong evidence to construct a complete explanatory model in Lesson 8. Formative assessment centres on the quality of the meal plan design and the accuracy of deficiency disease explanations as applied to the phenomenon children.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown a brief written case vignette (displayed on the board or read aloud) describing the brother's symptoms — tiredness, frequent illness, stunted growth, and swollen limbs — without yet naming any diseases. Working individually for 2 minutes, learners write their best prediction: 'Which specific nutrients do you think are missing from the brother's diet, and why do you think those symptoms appear?' They then share with a partner for 1 minute before a brief whole-class share-out. This activates prior knowledge from Lessons 1–6 and surfaces current understanding before new evidence is introduced.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the case vignette prominently. Say: 'You have been scientists for six lessons now — you know about proteins, carbohydrates, vitamins, minerals, and how the body digests and absorbs them. Look at these symptoms. Without turning to a neighbour yet, write down: which nutrient or nutrients do you think this boy is missing, and WHY do those specific symptoms appear?' Allow 2 minutes of individual silent writing. Then say: 'Now share your prediction with your partner. Try to challenge each other — is your partner's reasoning strong?' Allow 1 minute. Cold-call 3 pairs — choose one confident student, one hesitant student, and one mid-range student. After each response, say: 'What evidence from our previous lessons supports that prediction?' WAIT TIME: 12 seconds after posing the follow-up question before accepting responses. Record key predicted nutrients on the board in a 'Before' column — do not confirm or deny. Say: 'We will return to these predictions at the end of the lesson and see how well our science knowledge holds up.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Activation of Prior Knowledge through Symptom-to-Nutrient Mapping: By presenting symptoms before naming diseases, the teacher forces learners to reason from mechanism (what does this nutrient do?) to consequence (what happens when it is absent?), deepening conceptual understanding rather than encouraging rote recall of disease names.
