framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher listens for whether learners can name specific nutrients (e.g., protein, vitamin C, vitamin D, iron) rather than vague terms like 'vitamins.' The quality of causal reasoning ('swollen limbs happen because protein is needed to maintain osmotic balance in tissues') signals depth of prior learning. Students who cannot connect any symptom to a nutrient need targeted support during the Observe Phase jigsaw activity.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners engage in a structured Jigsaw Research Activity. The class is divided into six expert groups (or fewer groups covering multiple diseases if class is small), each assigned one deficiency disease: Group 1 — Kwashiorkor, Group 2 — Marasmus, Group 3 — Scurvy, Group 4 — Rickets, Group 5 — Anaemia, Group 6 — Night Blindness. Each group receives a one-page illustrated information card (prepared by the teacher) containing: the disease name, the missing nutrient(s), key symptoms with labelled diagrams or photos, which Kenyan foods contain the missing nutrient, and a real-world Kenyan prevalence note. Groups read, discuss, and complete a standardised evidence table (Disease | Missing Nutrient | Symptoms | Body System Affected | Kenyan Foods That Prevent It). After 7 minutes, groups reform as mixed 'teaching groups' where one expert from each disease group is present, and each expert teaches their disease to the group (2 minutes per disease, approximately 12 minutes total). Each learner fills in the full six-disease table as they listen.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute information cards and blank evidence tables. Say: 'Each group is now an expert team. Your job in the next 7 minutes is to understand your disease so deeply that you can teach it clearly to others. Use the information card, and make sure every member of your group can explain: the missing nutrient, the symptoms, and at least two Kenyan foods that would prevent this disease.' Circulate to each group during expert reading time. Prompt struggling groups with: 'What does this nutrient normally do in the body? So what goes wrong when it is missing?' After 7 minutes, restructure into mixed teaching groups. Say: 'You are no longer in your expert group. Each person at your new table must teach their disease. Listeners: fill in your evidence table as you learn. Ask questions — especially: does this connect to what we saw in the phenomenon?' During teaching rounds, cold-call 2 students at random after the jigsaw to check: 'Without looking at your notes, tell me — what is the missing nutrient in rickets, and name one Kenyan food that would help.' WAIT TIME: 10 seconds. Affirm accurate responses; gently correct errors by saying: 'Close — let's check the table together. What does the card say?'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Jigsaw Cooperative Learning with Structured Evidence Table: The jigsaw structure ensures every learner is both a teacher and a learner, promoting deep processing and accountability. The standardised evidence table scaffolds note-taking and creates a shared class reference that all learners will use in the Explain Phase. Teaching peers requires higher-order processing than passive reading.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks that each learner's completed evidence table contains accurate entries for all six diseases. During the jigsaw teach-back, listen for correct nutrient identification (not just disease name recall). A common error to watch for: confusing kwashiorkor (protein deficiency with adequate calories) and marasmus (total calorie and protein deficiency) — if heard, pause the group and ask: 'How are these two diseases different from each other, even though both involve poor nutrition?' This misconception directly connects to the phenomenon (the brother has calories from ugali but lacks protein — which disease does that suggest?).

framework.learnerExperience: Learners now apply their completed evidence tables directly to the phenomenon. Working in pairs, they complete a two-part critical evaluation task. Part A — Phenomenon Diagnosis: Learners write a 'nutritional diagnosis' of the brother in the phenomenon, identifying which deficiency diseases he is likely developing and explaining why, using evidence from their tables. They also evaluate the neighbour's child's diet (githeri, milk, sukuma wiki) and explain why it is more adequate. Part B — Affordable Balanced Meal Plan Design: Each pair designs a one-day meal plan (breakfast, lunch, and dinner) for a Kenyan family of four using locally available, affordable foods that would prevent all six deficiency diseases discussed. They must annotate each meal to show which nutrients it provides and which diseases it prevents. Pairs then share their meal plans with another pair and give feedback using the prompt: 'This meal plan covers [nutrient] well because… but it could be improved by adding… to address [disease].'
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Now we become doctors and nutritionists. Use your evidence table and everything you have learned since Lesson 1 to answer this: what is actually wrong with the boy in our phenomenon, scientifically? Do not just say he is malnourished — give him a specific nutritional diagnosis.' Allow 4 minutes for Part A. Cold-call 3 pairs to share their diagnoses. Ask: 'Which specific disease are you naming, and what is the nutrient evidence?' WAIT TIME: 12 seconds. For Part B, say: 'Now be practical. This family lives in Kericho. They have limited money. Design a one-day meal plan using foods available at a local market — think githeri, sweet potatoes, green vegetables, eggs, fish from Lake Victoria, beans, milk, fruits. Make sure your meals together cover proteins, carbohydrates, fats, vitamins A, C, D, iron, and calcium. Annotate every food item.' Allow 8 minutes for meal plan design. During peer feedback, prompt: 'Does your partner's plan have a source of vitamin C? Iron? What would you add, and why?' Bring class together and display 2–3 meal plans under the visualiser or on the board. Ask the class: 'Would these meal plans have kept the brother in our phenomenon healthy? What is the single most important change his family could make that is affordable?' Cold-call 4 students for responses. WAIT TIME: 10 seconds each.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Application Transfer with Annotated Meal Plan Design: Asking learners to design rather than merely identify forces application of multi-nutrient knowledge simultaneously. Annotation (labelling each food with nutrients and disease prevention) makes thinking visible and creates an artefact that reveals depth of understanding. Peer feedback using sentence starters structures productive scientific critique.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher collects or photographs meal plans. Look for: (1) variety across food groups rather than repetition of one food type; (2) accurate nutrient-to-food mapping (e.g., eggs and beans for protein, sukuma wiki for iron and vitamin A, citrus fruits for vitamin C, milk for calcium and vitamin D); (3) explicit connection to deficiency disease prevention. A strong response names at least four nutrient categories and links each to a specific local food. Weak responses that only list foods without annotation need follow-up questioning: 'Tell me what nutrient is in ugali and what disease does it prevent or not prevent?'

framework.learnerExperience: Learners individually write two sticky notes or journal entries: one green ('I can now explain…') and one yellow ('I still wonder…'). Green notes should address either of the two key inquiry questions using specific evidence from this lesson. Yellow notes capture remaining uncertainties or new questions generated by the lesson — for example, 'Why do some children in Kenya develop kwashiorkor even when they eat beans sometimes?' or 'How does the body actually absorb iron differently from calcium?' These are added to the class Driving Question Board. The class then reviews the board together, and the teacher facilitates a brief 3-minute discussion: 'Look at our DQB from Lesson 1. Which questions can we now fully answer? Which remain open for Lesson 8?'
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute sticky notes (or direct learners to their science journals). Say: 'Write one green note: start with the words — I can now explain why the brother in our phenomenon has [blank] because [blank]. Be specific — use a disease name, a nutrient, and a food. Write one yellow note: what is one thing you still wonder about, or a new question this lesson has raised for you?' Allow 3 minutes of silent writing. Invite 4 students to read their green notes aloud — specifically choose one student who struggled earlier to give them a moment of success. Then say: 'Now let's look at our Driving Question Board from the start of our unit. Point to a question we can now fully answer.' Cold-call 3 students. For each, ask: 'What evidence from today's lesson, or any lesson in our sequence, answers that question?' WAIT TIME: 12 seconds. For yellow notes, say: 'These remaining wonders are exactly what scientists feel — knowing more raises new questions. We will address the most important ones in Lesson 8 when we build our final explanatory model.' Physically add sticky notes to the DQB, grouping them by theme (nutrient gaps, disease-body connections, solutions).
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Green/Yellow Metacognitive Reflection combined with DQB Curation: The colour-coded reflection prompts learners to distinguish between consolidated understanding and productive uncertainty. Revisiting the DQB from Lesson 1 makes learning progress visible and motivates Lesson 8 by showing that some questions are now answerable while others deepen the investigation. This mirrors authentic scientific practice.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reads green notes to assess whether learners can correctly name a deficiency disease, link it to a nutrient, and connect it to the phenomenon with specificity. A strong green note example: 'I can now explain why the brother has kwashiorkor because he eats very little protein — no beans, eggs, or fish — and protein is needed for tissue repair and osmotic balance, which is why his limbs are swollen.' A weak note ('I can explain that the boy is sick because he lacks nutrients') signals surface-level understanding and that learner needs additional support in Lesson 8. Yellow notes revealing deep thinking (e.g., questions about absorption efficiency, food combinations, or poverty as a root cause) can be used to launch Lesson 8.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners retrieve their cumulative explanatory model from previous lessons (a diagram showing the digestive system, nutrient types, absorption, and transport to cells). They now add a 'Consequences Layer' to their model: using a different colour pen or pencil, they annotate what happens at the cellular and body system level when each major nutrient category is absent — protein deficiency leads to kwashiorkor (annotated near tissue cells), vitamin C deficiency leads to scurvy (annotated near connective tissue), iron deficiency leads to anaemia (annotated near blood/red blood cells), vitamin A deficiency leads to night blindness (annotated near the eye), vitamin D and calcium deficiency leads to rickets (annotated near bones), and total energy deficiency leads to marasmus (annotated near all body systems). Learners also add a 'Solutions Arrow' from the phenomenon brother to a revised diet showing the specific foods that would address his deficiencies. They share their revised models with a partner and explain one annotation verbally.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Take out your cumulative model. Today we add the final science layer — consequences. Use a coloured pen — different from what you used before — and annotate your model. For each nutrient we have studied, show what goes wrong when it is missing. Connect each consequence to the correct part of the body in your diagram. Then add an arrow showing: if the brother in our phenomenon changed his diet to include [specific foods], which diseases would he prevent?' Allow 6 minutes for model annotation. Circulate and ask targeted questions: 'You have annotated kwashiorkor near the muscles — can you also show me why the limbs swell? What is the role of protein in fluid balance?' and 'Where on your model does iron deficiency cause problems — show me the specific organ or process.' After 6 minutes, say: 'Share your model with your partner. Partner: point to one annotation and ask your partner to explain it out loud, without reading from the model.' Cold-call 2 pairs to share one annotation and explanation with the class. Close by saying: 'In Lesson 8, you will use this model to build your final, complete explanation of the phenomenon. You are almost there — look at how much science is now in your model compared to Lesson 1.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision with Consequences Layer: Adding a visually distinct 'consequences layer' to an existing model externalises the causal chain from nutrient absence to disease outcome, making abstract biochemical consequences spatially visible. The verbal explanation to a partner (without reading) tests whether the model represents genuine understanding or copied annotations — a key distinction in competency-based assessment.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher circulates to check that annotations are causally accurate (not just labelling disease names next to body parts, but showing the mechanistic link — e.g., 'No vitamin C → collagen not synthesised → connective tissue breaks down → scurvy'). Learners who can explain their annotations verbally without reading demonstrate deep understanding. Learners who annotate correctly but cannot explain the mechanism need guiding questions: 'Why does that happen there? What does that nutrient normally do at that location?' Collect 5–6 model books as an exit-ticket check — focus on whether the 'Solutions Arrow' shows specific Kenyan foods linked to specific nutrients and diseases, which demonstrates full integration of lesson learning.

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
1. Were learners able to accurately name and explain all six deficiency diseases, or were there specific diseases (e.g., distinguishing kwashiorkor from marasmus) that caused persistent confusion — and how will I address those gaps in Lesson 8?
2. Did the jigsaw structure ensure equitable participation, or did certain learners dominate while others remained passive? What would I adjust in the jigsaw grouping or time allocation?
3. How well did learners' meal plans reflect genuine integration of nutritional knowledge versus surface-level food listing? Were the meal plans both nutritionally adequate AND realistic for a Kenyan family's budget and food access?
4. Did learners make strong explicit connections between the phenomenon (the brother and the neighbour's child) and the deficiency diseases, or did they treat the diseases as abstract content disconnected from the storyline?
5. Looking at the DQB yellow notes, what unresolved questions or misconceptions are most prevalent, and how will I ensure these are addressed in Lesson 8's final model-building and sense-making session?

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners researched and taught each other six deficiency diseases — kwashiorkor, marasmus, scurvy, rickets, anaemia, and night blindness — identifying the missing nutrient, symptoms, affected body systems, and Kenyan foods that prevent each disease. They critically evaluated the phenomenon children's diets and designed affordable balanced meal plans using local Kenyan foods including githeri, sukuma wiki, milk, beans, eggs, and fruits.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Eating sufficient quantities of food does not guarantee good health — the type and variety of food consumed determines whether the body receives all essential nutrients. When specific nutrients are chronically absent, predictable, nameable diseases result: protein deficiency causes kwashiorkor or marasmus; vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy; vitamin D/calcium deficiency causes rickets; iron deficiency causes anaemia; and vitamin A deficiency causes night blindness. A balanced diet requires variety across all nutrient groups, which is achievable using affordable, locally available Kenyan foods.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The brother in the phenomenon is almost certainly developing kwashiorkor and possibly multiple micronutrient deficiencies (anaemia, night blindness) because his diet of predominantly ugali provides calories (carbohydrates) but is severely lacking in protein, iron, vitamin A, and vitamin C — all of which are absent when beans, eggs, fish, and vegetables are rarely eaten. His swollen limbs, tiredness, frequent illness, and stunted growth are not random — they are the specific, predictable consequences of those nutrient deficiencies. The neighbour's child is healthy because githeri, milk, and sukuma wiki together provide a broader range of nutrients including protein, calcium, iron, and vitamins, even in smaller quantities. This fully confirms that food quality and variety matter as much as — and in this case more than — food quantity.

LESSONS.title: Synthesising Nutrition in Animals: Model Building, DQB Review and Final Evidence-Based Explanation
LESSONS.aresKeywords: digestion, absorption, assimilation, egestion, balanced diet, malnutrition, deficiency diseases, nutrients, enzymes, villi, holozoic nutrition

slo.purpose: By the end of this lesson, learners will have synthesised all unit learning into a complete, annotated model of the human digestive system and produced a final evidence-based written explanation of the anchoring phenomenon, demonstrating mastery of nutrition in animals.
slo.knowledge: Learners will be able to: (1) describe the complete journey of nutrients from ingestion to assimilation and egestion; (2) explain how the absence of specific nutrients in a diet leads to named deficiency diseases; (3) distinguish between eating enough food in quantity versus eating a nutritionally adequate diet.
slo.skills: Learners will demonstrate ability to: (1) construct and annotate a cumulative model integrating mechanical digestion, chemical digestion, enzyme action, absorption via villi, and egestion; (2) use evidence from food tests, digestive system diagrams, and malnutrition data to justify claims in a written explanation; (3) critically evaluate a dietary scenario and propose improvements using locally available Kenyan foods.
slo.attitudes: Learners will appreciate that scientific understanding of nutrition has practical relevance to the health of their own families and communities, and will demonstrate responsibility by applying this knowledge to real dietary decisions.
slo.keyInquiry: Why do two children who both eat enough food every day end up with very different health outcomes, and what actually happens inside the body when we eat a meal of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans?
slo.purposeInStoryline: This is the final synthesis lesson. Learners have progressed from puzzlement at the phenomenon, through classification of nutrients, food testing, study of digestive organs, enzyme action, absorption, and malnutrition. Now they integrate every piece of evidence into a complete explanatory model and a final written response to the driving question, closing the inquiry loop they began in Lesson 1.
slo.safety8otes: No hazardous materials are used in this lesson. Ensure learners handle printed diagrams and model-building stationery (scissors, glue, markers) safely. If digital devices are in use, supervise appropriate use.

LESSONS.overview: Learners open by briefly revisiting their Lesson 1 predictive models of what food does in the body, immediately activating awareness of how far their thinking has advanced. They then complete and annotate their cumulative digestive system models, working in groups to integrate all unit content: nutrient types, mechanical and chemical digestion, enzyme specificity, villus absorption, large intestine water reabsorption, and egestion. Groups present their models in a gallery walk format. The class then returns to the Driving Question Board to systematically answer every question posted across the unit, using evidence from lessons 1 through 7. The lesson culminates in an individual written explanation of the anchoring phenomenon, serving as the summative assessment for the sub-strand.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners receive back their original Lesson 1 predictive model drawings or written predictions about what food does in the body. They read their original ideas silently for 2 minutes. The teacher then poses the question: Look at what you wrote or drew in Lesson 1. What did you think was happening inside the body when you eat ugali and beans? What would you change or add now? Learners annotate their original models in a different colour pen or sticky note, writing 2 to 3 specific things they now know that they did not know in Lesson 1. This activates metacognitive awareness of learning progression and reconnects to the phenomenon.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute Lesson 1 models or prediction sheets before learners sit down so there is no transition time lost. Say: Without looking at your notes, read what you predicted in Lesson 1 about why the two children had different health outcomes. Give 2 minutes of SILENT WAIT TIME. Then say: Now annotate your original prediction in a different colour. Write at least two specific pieces of evidence you now have that you did not have then. Circulate and listen for misconceptions that persist. Probe with: You said the food just gives energy back then. What do you know now that is more precise than that? Use a show of hands to ask: Who has completely changed their original prediction? Who has partly changed it? Who thinks they were mostly right from the start? Acknowledge all responses without judgment, then say: By the end of today, every one of you will be able to answer the driving question with evidence from eight lessons of investigation.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Revisiting and annotating original predictions makes prior-to-now learning visible. It positions the synthesis lesson as a payoff for the whole unit rather than simply another lesson, and motivates engagement with the model-building and writing tasks that follow.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans annotated predictions to identify any learners who are still at surface-level changes (for example, only adding vocabulary without conceptual change). These learners are flagged for additional support during the model-building phase. Listen for correct use of terms such as deficiency, enzyme, absorption, and villi as indicators of vocabulary integration.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are given a large blank outline diagram of the human digestive system (A3 size or drawn on sugar paper) that includes unlabelled organs from the mouth to the anus, plus boxes for the liver and pancreas. Working in groups of 4, they are given a set of pre-cut information cards (prepared by the teacher in advance) covering: (1) the nutrient each organ processes, (2) the enzyme or secretion produced, (3) the type of digestion occurring, and (4) the product of digestion at each stage. Groups must place each card at the correct organ, then annotate the diagram with arrows showing the direction of food movement and colour-coding to show where carbohydrates, proteins, and lipids are each broken down. The ugali-sukuma wiki-beans meal from the phenomenon is explicitly referenced: learners label what happens to the starch from ugali, the protein from beans, the iron from sukuma wiki, and the vitamins from sukuma wiki at each organ. This is an observed record of the complete digestive process.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute materials efficiently: one A3 diagram per group, one set of pre-cut information cards per group, coloured markers. Say: Your job is to build the most complete and accurate model of what happens to a meal of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans from the moment it enters the mouth until waste leaves the body. Every card must be placed and every organ labelled. You have 10 minutes. Set a visible timer. Circulate actively. At the 5-minute mark, prompt: Have you included what happens to vitamins and minerals from sukuma wiki? Where does iron get absorbed? Where does the protein from beans first get chemically digested? Challenge higher-attaining groups: Can you show on your diagram the difference between what happens to a child eating only ugali versus a child eating ugali, beans, and sukuma wiki? Use specific probing: What does the villus do that the stomach cannot do? Why does the large intestine matter even though it does not digest food? Allow groups to compare cards with neighbouring groups if uncertain, modelling peer-checking as a scientific practice.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The card-sorting and diagram annotation activity externalises thinking, making it visible to the teacher and to peers. Using the actual foods from the phenomenon as the content of the model keeps the investigation anchored to the real-world puzzle throughout. Colour-coding by nutrient type builds visual pattern recognition across the complexity of the full digestive process.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks each group diagram for: correct placement of salivary amylase in the mouth, pepsin in the stomach, lipase and trypsin in the small intestine; correct identification of the small intestine as the primary site of absorption; correct location of the liver producing bile and the pancreas producing multiple enzymes; correct annotation of the large intestine for water reabsorption. Common errors to watch for include placing absorption in the stomach, omitting the role of bile, and confusing mechanical and chemical digestion.

framework.learnerExperience: Groups conduct a 6-minute gallery walk. Each group leaves their completed model on the desk and members rotate clockwise to view two other groups models. Learners use sticky notes to leave one specific praise comment (something correct and well-explained they observe) and one probing question (something they are unsure about or want the other group to explain further). Groups return to their own model, read the sticky notes, and spend 3 minutes discussing and resolving any questions raised. The teacher then facilitates a 5-minute whole-class synthesis discussion. One representative from each group shares the most important single annotation on their model: the moment in digestion they think best explains why the child eating only ugali is unhealthy. This drives the class toward articulating the core explanation: it is not lack of calories but lack of specific nutrients, each of which serves specific functions, and whose absence causes specific, predictable diseases.
framework.teacherMoves: Before the gallery walk, say: You are visiting other scientists exhibits. Leave a genuine praise note and a genuine question note on every model you visit. Be specific. Do not write good job. Write what exactly is good and why. During the gallery walk, circulate and read sticky notes as they are posted, redirecting vague feedback with: Can you be more specific? What exactly on that diagram shows that? After groups return to their own models, ask the whole class: Without using the word food, explain in one sentence why Amina from Kericho is healthy and her brother is not. Give 30 seconds of SILENT WAIT TIME. Take 3 to 4 responses. Press for precision: You said she eats the right things. What does right mean scientifically? Guide toward: she consumes all essential nutrient groups in adequate quantities, enabling all metabolic processes to function correctly. Write this refined statement on the board and tell learners: This is the core scientific claim your final writing must be built on.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The gallery walk uses peer feedback as a learning mechanism rather than just a presentation ritual. The requirement to leave specific praise and a question forces evaluative reading of other groups work. The whole-class synthesis narrows multiple group responses to a shared, precise scientific claim that will anchor the individual written assessment.
framework.formativeAssessment: Listen during the gallery walk discussion for whether learners can connect specific nutrients to specific organs and specific diseases. Red-flag statements include: the body just uses all food the same way or any food gives you energy so it does not matter what you eat. If heard, pause the class briefly and redirect: Let us test that. If all food gives energy equally, why does the child with only ugali have swollen limbs and not just low energy? What specific nutrient does that tell us is missing?

framework.learnerExperience: The class DQB (created and added to across Lessons 1 through 7) is displayed at the front. Each question posted over the unit is read aloud by the student who originally posted it, where possible. Working as a class, learners collaboratively decide: (1) whether the question has now been fully answered, (2) whether it has been partially answered, or (3) whether it remains genuinely open. For questions that are fully answered, a nominated learner states the evidence-based answer in 1 to 2 sentences and the question card is marked with a green tick. Partially answered questions receive an orange dot and a brief note of what is still uncertain. Questions that remain open (for example, questions about the gut microbiome, genetic factors in nutrient absorption, or specific Kenyan public health data) are noted as areas for further learning beyond this unit. This process typically takes 5 to 6 minutes given the 40-minute constraint, so the teacher pre-selects 5 to 6 of the most central DQB questions to address in full, with remaining questions reviewed briefly.
framework.teacherMoves: Before the DQB review, say: In Lesson 1, you were puzzled. You asked questions you did not yet have the tools to answer. Let us find out how far we have come. Read each pre-selected DQB question aloud clearly. Ask: Who posted this? Can you now answer it using evidence from our lessons? If the original poster cannot answer, open to the class. For each answered question, press for completeness: Is that a full answer or a partial answer? What evidence supports it? Specifically prompt for connections to the phenomenon: How does this answer help us explain what is happening to Amina and her brother in Kericho? For any question that remains genuinely open, validate the inquiry: That is a real scientific question that researchers at institutions like the Kenya Medical Research Institute are actively studying. Not knowing is not a failure. It is the start of further science. End the DQB review by saying: Every question you posted in Lesson 1 came from genuine curiosity about a real health puzzle. The fact that you can now answer most of them with evidence means you have done real scientific thinking over this unit.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Returning to the DQB makes the cumulative nature of the unit visible and gives learners a concrete record of their intellectual journey. It validates curiosity as a scientific tool and models the difference between answered questions, partially understood questions, and genuinely open research questions, building epistemological sophistication.
framework.formativeAssessment: Note which questions learners can answer confidently and which ones still produce hesitancy or incorrect responses. Questions about enzyme specificity and the difference between mechanical and chemical digestion frequently remain partially understood at this stage. If so, briefly clarify using the annotated model on the desk before moving to the writing task. Do not re-teach whole concepts at this stage; target specific confusions in 30 to 60 seconds.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners individually write a final evidence-based explanation of the anchoring phenomenon (8 to 10 minutes). The prompt is displayed on the board: A student from Kericho notices that her brother eats large quantities of ugali daily but is tired, sick often, has stunted growth, and has swollen limbs. Her neighbour eats smaller but more varied meals including githeri, milk, and sukuma wiki, and is healthy and energetic. Using evidence from your investigations across this unit, explain why these two children have such different health outcomes. Your explanation must include: (1) the role of nutrient variety versus quantity, (2) how the digestive system processes at least two specific nutrients, (3) the name and cause of at least one deficiency disease observable in the brother, and (4) a recommendation for improving the brothers diet using affordable Kenyan foods. Learners write independently. The completed written explanation serves as the primary summative assessment for Sub-strand 3.1. After writing, learners are invited to make one final revision to their Lesson 1 model, this time without any constraints, to show their fullest current understanding. These final models are collected alongside the written explanations.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the writing prompt clearly and read it aloud once. Say: This is your moment to show everything you have learned. Use your annotated model on the desk. Use your notes. Use the evidence from every lesson. You have 8 minutes to write. Silence, please, so everyone can think. Enforce quiet writing time — this is an assessment moment. At the 6-minute mark, give a quiet reminder: Two minutes remaining. Make sure you have included a recommendation using real Kenyan foods. After writing, say: Spend the last 2 minutes going back to your very first prediction from Lesson 1 and making any final revision that shows your thinking now. Use a different colour. After collecting work, close the lesson by saying: In Lesson 1, you were puzzled by two children eating enough food but having completely different health. You have now done what scientists do: you observed carefully, you tested hypotheses, you built models, you used evidence to explain a real-world phenomenon. The answer to the driving question is not simple — it involves nutrients, digestion, enzymes, absorption, and the consequences of imbalance. That complexity is exactly why nutrition science matters for every family in Kenya.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The individual written explanation is the culminating sensemaking task, requiring learners to integrate all unit content into a coherent, evidence-based narrative. Referencing the specific Kenyan context (Kericho, ugali, githeri, sukuma wiki) throughout the prompt ensures the science remains connected to lived experience rather than becoming abstract. Returning to the Lesson 1 prediction as a final step makes the metacognitive arc of the unit tangible and personally meaningful.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect written explanations and final models. Use a 4-point rubric: (1) Nutrient variety vs quantity — does the learner clearly distinguish between caloric sufficiency and nutritional adequacy? (2) Digestive process — does the learner accurately describe what happens to at least two specific nutrients during digestion, naming at least one enzyme? (3) Deficiency disease — does the learner correctly name and explain a disease visible in the brother, most likely kwashiorkor (swollen limbs indicating protein deficiency)? (4) Dietary recommendation — does the learner suggest specific, named, affordable Kenyan foods that address the identified deficiency? Learners who meet all four criteria with accurate science demonstrate mastery. Those meeting 2 to 3 criteria demonstrate partial mastery and require targeted follow-up. Those meeting fewer than 2 criteria require re-engagement with core content before progression.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, consider the following: Did all learners successfully connect the swollen limbs in the phenomenon to kwashiorkor and protein deficiency specifically, or did some remain at a general level of he lacks nutrients? Were learners able to name specific enzymes and their sites of action in their written explanations, or did they describe digestion in general terms without enzyme-level precision? Did the gallery walk generate genuine peer feedback, or did sticky notes remain superficial? Were there DQB questions that the class could not adequately answer, suggesting gaps in coverage across lessons 1 to 7 that should be addressed in future iterations? Consider whether the 40-minute duration was sufficient for quality individual writing — if time is consistently insufficient, the DQB review could be partially completed in Lesson 7 to free up more writing time in Lesson 8. Reflect also on whether the Kenyan food context remained genuinely central throughout the unit or whether it was applied only at the surface level of examples.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: We observed that a child eating large quantities of ugali alone shows signs of protein deficiency (swollen limbs, stunted growth, fatigue) while a child eating smaller but varied meals including githeri, sukuma wiki, and milk is healthy and energetic. We observed our own Lesson 1 predictions and how they changed across 8 lessons of investigation.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: We learned that eating enough food in quantity is not the same as eating a nutritionally adequate diet. The type and variety of food consumed determines whether the body receives all essential nutrients — carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, vitamins, minerals, water, and fibre — each of which serves specific metabolic roles. The digestive system processes each nutrient type using specific mechanical processes and specific enzymes at specific organs, and absorbs digested nutrients through villi in the small intestine into the bloodstream for assimilation by body cells. Chronic absence of any single nutrient group causes predictable, named deficiency diseases.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: We explained that Amina from Kericho is healthy because her varied diet supplies all essential nutrients, enabling her digestive system to absorb and her body cells to assimilate the full range of substances needed for growth, immune function, and energy metabolism. Her brother is unhealthy despite eating large amounts because ugali supplies mainly carbohydrates but lacks adequate protein, iron, vitamins A and C, and other micronutrients. His swollen limbs indicate kwashiorkor caused by severe protein deficiency. The digestive system cannot manufacture missing nutrients — it can only process what is consumed. This is why diet variety matters as much as diet quantity, and why affordable diversification of Kenyan meals using locally available foods like beans, sukuma wiki, eggs, and milk directly determines health outcomes.

FINAL_EXPLANATION.instructions: This Final Explanation assessment asks you to bring together everything you have learned across all eight lessons in the sub-strand 'Nutrition in Animals.' You will answer each section using scientific evidence, vocabulary, and reasoning developed during our investigations. Your responses must directly address the driving question: Why do two children who both eat 'enough food' every day end up with very different health outcomes — and what actually happens inside the body when we eat a meal of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans? Write in full sentences and paragraphs. Use specific scientific terms you have learned (for example: carbohydrates, proteins, vitamins, minerals, enzymes, villi, peristalsis, deficiency diseases). Support your explanations with evidence from our class experiments, observations, and readings. Where possible, connect your answers back to the two children from Kericho whose situation launched our entire investigation. Your teacher will assess your work using the rubric at the end of this document, so read each section prompt carefully before writing. You have 60 minutes to complete this assessment. Work independently and show the full depth of your understanding.

sections.title: SECTION 1: NUTRIENTS — WHAT THEY ARE AND WHY THE BODY NEEDS THEM
sections.prompt: Identify the major classes of nutrients found in food. For each class, state its main function in the body and give at least one example of a Kenyan food source that provides it. Then explain why the brother from Kericho, who eats mostly ugali, is likely missing critical nutrients — and predict which specific deficiency diseases or health problems he is at risk of developing as a result.
sections.exemplar:
The six major classes of nutrients are carbohydrates, proteins, lipids (fats and oils), vitamins, minerals, and water. Carbohydrates are the body's primary source of energy; ugali, which is made from maize flour, is rich in starch, a complex carbohydrate. Proteins are needed for growth, repair of body tissues, and the production of enzymes and hormones; good Kenyan sources include beans, eggs, fish from Lake Victoria, and milk. Lipids provide a concentrated store of energy, insulate the body, and carry fat-soluble vitamins; they are found in foods such as avocado and cooking oil. Vitamins and minerals, though needed in small quantities, are essential for specific body functions — for example, vitamin C from sukuma wiki supports the immune system, iron from beans carries oxygen in haemoglobin, and calcium from milk strengthens bones. Water makes up about 60% of the body and is involved in every chemical reaction.

The brother from Kericho eats large quantities of ugali but rarely consumes beans, eggs, fish, or vegetables. This means his diet is heavily dominated by carbohydrates and is severely lacking in proteins, most vitamins, and key minerals. Despite eating what feels like 'enough food' in terms of bulk and energy, his body is starved of the building blocks it needs for growth and immune defence. The swollen limbs observed in the phenomenon are a classic sign of kwashiorkor, a protein-deficiency disease in which low blood protein causes fluid to accumulate in tissues (oedema). His frequent illnesses suggest vitamin C and zinc deficiency, which weakens immune responses. His stunted growth compared to classmates points to both protein and overall micronutrient deficiency. His fatigue is consistent with iron-deficiency anaemia, since without adequate protein and iron, haemoglobin cannot be produced in sufficient quantities to carry oxygen to body cells efficiently. This illustrates why the type and variety of food matters just as much as the quantity consumed.

sections.title: SECTION 2: THE HUMAN DIGESTIVE SYSTEM — STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION
sections.prompt: Describe the journey of a meal of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans through the human digestive system, from the moment it enters the mouth to the point where nutrients are absorbed into the bloodstream. Name the organs involved in each stage, explain the role of digestive enzymes and other secretions, and describe the physical and chemical processes that occur at each stage.
sections.exemplar:
When a person sits down to eat ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans, digestion begins immediately in the mouth. The teeth mechanically break the food into smaller pieces, increasing the surface area for enzyme action — this is physical digestion. At the same time, salivary glands release saliva containing the enzyme salivary amylase, which begins the chemical digestion of starch in the ugali, breaking it down into shorter chains of maltose. The tongue shapes the food into a bolus, which is swallowed and pushed down the oesophagus by a wave of muscular contractions called peristalsis.

In the stomach, the food is churned vigorously by muscular walls, mixing it with gastric juice. Gastric juice contains hydrochloric acid, which creates an acidic environment (pH 2) that kills most bacteria and activates the enzyme pepsin. Pepsin begins the chemical digestion of proteins from the beans, breaking them into shorter polypeptide chains. The semi-liquid mixture produced, called chyme, is released in small amounts into the small intestine through the pyloric sphincter. The small intestine is the main site of both digestion and absorption. Here, bile from the liver (stored in the gall bladder) emulsifies fats from any oil in the meal, increasing their surface area. Pancreatic juice from the pancreas delivers amylase (continuing starch digestion), lipase (completing fat digestion into fatty acids and glycerol), and proteases such as trypsin (continuing protein digestion into amino acids). Intestinal enzymes on the wall of the small intestine complete digestion — for example, maltase converts maltose to glucose. The resulting nutrients — glucose, amino acids, fatty acids, glycerol, vitamins, and minerals — are absorbed across the highly folded inner wall of the small intestine. The folds, villi, and microvilli dramatically increase the surface area for absorption. Glucose and amino acids pass directly into the capillaries of the blood, while fatty acids and glycerol enter the lacteal lymph vessels. Water and mineral salts are then absorbed in the large intestine, and undigested material is eliminated as faeces through the rectum and anus.

sections.title: SECTION 3: BALANCED DIET AND THE CONSEQUENCES OF DIETARY IMBALANCE
sections.prompt: Define the term 'balanced diet' and explain what it means in the context of Kenyan eating patterns. Compare the diets of the two children described in the phenomenon — the brother from Kericho (mainly ugali) and the neighbour's child (githeri, milk, sukuma wiki). Use nutritional evidence to explain why one child is healthy and the other is malnourished, even though both eat 'enough food.' Suggest realistic, affordable dietary improvements for the brother's family that are appropriate for a rural Kenyan household.
sections.exemplar:
A balanced diet is one that contains all the essential nutrients — carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins, minerals, and water — in the correct proportions to meet the body's needs for energy, growth, repair, and regulation of body processes. It is not simply about eating large quantities of food; it is about eating the right variety of foods in appropriate amounts. In Kenya, a balanced diet does not have to be expensive — it can be built around locally available, affordable foods.

Comparing the two children makes the concept of dietary balance very clear. The brother from Kericho consumes mainly ugali, which is high in carbohydrates and provides energy, but offers very little protein, negligible vitamins, and minimal minerals. His diet is energy-sufficient but nutritionally incomplete — a condition sometimes called 'hidden hunger.' The neighbour's child, by contrast, eats githeri (a mixture of maize and beans), which provides both carbohydrates and plant protein, sukuma wiki, which supplies vitamins A, C, and K as well as iron and calcium, and milk, which adds animal protein, calcium, and vitamin B12. This varied diet, even in smaller quantities, covers the full spectrum of nutritional needs, supporting healthy immune function, normal growth, and sustained energy levels.

For the brother's family, realistic and affordable dietary improvements could include: adding beans, lentils, or groundnuts to daily meals as cheap protein sources; growing sukuma wiki or other leafy vegetables in a kitchen garden (common in rural Kericho); incorporating eggs from locally kept chickens; eating small dried fish (omena from Lake Victoria), which are inexpensive and rich in protein, calcium, and omega-3 fats; and drinking uji (porridge) made from mixed flours to increase micronutrient variety. These changes do not require significant extra expense — they require a change in dietary knowledge and prioritisation.

sections.title: SECTION 4: CONNECTING DIGESTION TO HEALTH OUTCOMES — EXPLAINING THE PHENOMENON
sections.prompt: Return directly to the phenomenon of the two children from Kericho. Using your full understanding of nutrients and digestion, construct a complete scientific explanation for why the brother who eats large amounts of ugali ends up with swollen limbs, frequent illness, stunted growth, and fatigue — while the neighbour's child who eats smaller but more varied meals is healthy and growing well. Your explanation must link what is eaten → how it is digested → what nutrients are absorbed → what the body can and cannot do with those nutrients → the observable health outcomes.
sections.exemplar:
The key to explaining the contrasting health outcomes of the two children lies in understanding that the body can only build and maintain itself using the raw materials delivered by digestion and absorption. When a person eats ugali, the digestive system breaks down the starch into glucose through enzymatic action (salivary amylase in the mouth, pancreatic amylase in the small intestine), and this glucose is absorbed into the bloodstream and used for energy. However, if the meal contains almost no protein, the body cannot produce the amino acids needed to build and repair muscle tissue, synthesise enzymes and hormones, produce antibodies for immune defence, or make haemoglobin for red blood cells. Fat-soluble vitamins cannot be absorbed without dietary fat, and without iron, vitamin C, and folate from vegetables, blood production and immune function are further compromised.

For the brother who eats mainly ugali, the digestive system is functioning correctly — it faithfully breaks down and absorbs whatever nutrients are present. The problem is that the food itself does not contain sufficient protein, vitamins, or minerals to supply the body with what it needs. With very low protein absorption, his body cannot maintain normal blood protein (albumin) levels, and as a result, fluid leaks from blood vessels into surrounding tissues, causing the visible swelling in his limbs — the hallmark of kwashiorkor. Without enough iron and protein for haemoglobin synthesis, his blood carries less oxygen, leaving him chronically fatigued. Without vitamin C, zinc, and protein for antibody production, his immune system cannot respond effectively to infections, explaining his frequent illnesses. Without amino acids as building blocks, his body cannot grow at the normal rate, resulting in stunted height compared to peers.

The neighbour's child, eating githeri, sukuma wiki, and milk, delivers a full complement of nutrients through digestion. Protein from beans and milk is digested to amino acids that are absorbed through the villi into the blood and transported to cells for growth and repair. Iron from sukuma wiki, vitamin C (which enhances iron absorption) also from sukuma wiki, and calcium from milk all enter the bloodstream and support haemoglobin production, immune function, and bone development respectively. The result is a child who is energetic, healthy, and growing normally — not because she eats more, but because what she eats, after digestion, gives her body everything it needs to function. This is the core scientific answer to the driving question: it is not the quantity of food that determines health outcomes, but the quality, variety, and nutritional completeness of the diet, combined with the body's ability to digest and absorb those nutrients at the cellular level.

sections.title: SECTION 5: REFLECTION — SCIENCE, COMMUNITY, AND YOUR OWN THINKING
sections.prompt: Reflect on how your understanding has changed since the beginning of this unit. What did you think at the start about why the brother from Kericho was unhealthy? What do you understand now that you did not understand before? Identify one misconception you held that the evidence changed, and explain how it changed. Finally, describe one action you could take in your own community — your school, home, or village — to apply what you have learned about nutrition to improve health outcomes.
sections.exemplar:
At the start of this unit, I thought that as long as someone eats enough food to feel full and not be hungry, they would be healthy. The case of the brother from Kericho was confusing to me because he was clearly eating a lot of ugali — large portions every day — so I initially assumed his health problems must have a different cause, perhaps an infection or something genetic. I did not connect his diet directly to his swollen limbs, tiredness, or slow growth.

Now I understand that the quantity of food is only one part of the picture. What matters equally — and perhaps more — is the nutritional composition of the food and whether it provides the full range of nutrients the body needs. I learned that the digestive system is essentially a nutrient-extraction machine: it breaks down food into absorbable molecules, but it can only extract and absorb what is actually present in the food. If a diet lacks protein, no amount of efficient digestion can produce amino acids from ugali alone. The specific misconception I held was that 'more food equals better nutrition.' The evidence from our food testing experiments (using Benedict's solution for sugars and the biuret test for proteins), our study of deficiency diseases, and our analysis of the two children's diets all demonstrated clearly that this is false. A small, varied meal of githeri and sukuma wiki is nutritionally superior to a large plate of ugali alone.

In my community, I would start a conversation with parents and caregivers — perhaps through the school's parents' meeting — about the importance of dietary variety. Many families in Kenya grow maize and have access to beans, sukuma wiki, and omena, but may not realise how powerfully these foods complement each other nutritionally. I would share what I learned about kwashiorkor and iron-deficiency anaemia, using the story of the two children from Kericho as a relatable example, to show that small, affordable changes — adding a handful of beans to ugali, growing sukuma wiki in the compound, buying omena at the local market — can make a profound difference to a child's health and future.

rubric.criterion: Scientific Accuracy and Use of Key Concepts
rubric.excellent: Excellent (4): Accurately and consistently uses all key scientific terms (nutrients, enzymes, villi, peristalsis, deficiency diseases, etc.). Correctly identifies nutrient classes, their functions, food sources, and the stages and processes of digestion with no factual errors. Demonstrates deep understanding of how nutrient absorption links to body function and health outcomes.
rubric.proficient: Proficient (3): Uses most key scientific terms correctly with minor errors. Correctly describes the majority of nutrient classes and digestive processes. Shows solid understanding of the link between diet, digestion, and health, with occasional imprecision in detail.
rubric.developing: Developing (1-2): Uses few scientific terms or uses them incorrectly. Shows limited or confused understanding of nutrient classes, digestion, or the relationship between diet and health outcomes. Contains significant factual errors that obscure understanding.

rubric.excellent: Excellent (4): Constructs a complete, coherent, and detailed scientific explanation that explicitly links what is eaten → digestion → absorption → cellular function → observable health outcomes for both children in the phenomenon. Clearly explains why quantity alone does not determine health, using multiple lines of nutritional and digestive evidence.
rubric.proficient: Proficient (3): Provides a mostly complete explanation of the phenomenon that connects diet to health outcomes for both children. Makes the link between digestion and health clear, though some steps in the causal chain may be less fully developed. Addresses the driving question with reasonable depth.
rubric.developing: Developing (1-2): Provides a partial or superficial explanation of the phenomenon. May describe symptoms without fully explaining their nutritional or digestive causes. The link between what is eaten, how it is digested, and the resulting health outcome is unclear or missing.

rubric.criterion: Use of Evidence and Scientific Reasoning
rubric.excellent: Excellent (4): Consistently supports claims with specific evidence from class experiments (e.g., food tests, enzyme investigations), observations, and readings. Reasoning is logical, well-structured, and explicitly connects evidence to conclusions. Demonstrates NGSS practices of constructing explanations and using evidence.
rubric.proficient: Proficient (3): Supports most claims with relevant evidence from lessons. Reasoning is generally logical, though some conclusions may not be fully justified by the evidence cited. Shows ability to connect observations to broader scientific principles.
rubric.developing: Developing (1-2): Makes claims with little or no supporting evidence from lessons or experiments. Reasoning is superficial, circular, or absent. Relies on general statements rather than specific scientific evidence to justify conclusions.

rubric.criterion: Application to Kenyan Context and Real-Life Relevance
rubric.excellent: Excellent (4): Consistently uses specific Kenyan food examples, place names, and community contexts throughout all sections. Dietary recommendations are realistic, affordable, and clearly appropriate for a Kenyan rural context. Demonstrates genuine understanding of how nutrition science applies to local lived experience.
rubric.proficient: Proficient (3): Uses Kenyan food examples and context in most sections. Dietary recommendations are generally relevant to Kenyan contexts, though they may occasionally lack specificity or local applicability. Shows awareness of how science connects to community health.
rubric.developing: Developing (1-2): Makes little or no use of Kenyan food examples or local context. Recommendations may be generic, impractical, or disconnected from the realities of Kenyan rural households. Limited demonstration of how the science connects to local community life.

rubric.excellent: Excellent (4): Writing is clear, well-organised, and uses precise scientific vocabulary throughout. Arguments are presented in a logical sequence with a clear introduction of ideas, supporting detail, and well-drawn conclusions. All sections are fully addressed with appropriate depth and length.
rubric.proficient: Proficient (3): Writing is generally clear and organised, with adequate use of scientific vocabulary. Most arguments follow a logical sequence, though some sections may be less fully developed or less precisely worded. All sections are addressed.
rubric.developing: Developing (1-2): Writing is unclear, poorly organised, or uses very limited scientific vocabulary. Arguments are difficult to follow or incomplete. One or more sections may be missing or addressed only superficially.

SUMMARY_TABLE.drivingQuestion: DRIVING QUESTION: Why do two children who both eat 'enough food' every day end up with very different health outcomes — and what actually happens inside the body when we eat a meal of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans?

lessons.title: Anchoring the Mystery: Why Do Two Children Eating 'Enough Food' Have Such Different Health Outcomes?
lessons.observed: Students examined two case studies of Kenyan children — both eating 'enough food' daily — but with strikingly different health outcomes (one healthy and active, the other showing fatigue, swollen limbs, and poor concentration). Students also surveyed their own typical daily meals and categorised foods by type.
lessons.learned: Eating 'enough food' by quantity does not guarantee good health. The TYPE, VARIETY, and BALANCE of food consumed determines health outcomes. Food can be grouped into broad categories (energy-giving, body-building, protective), and the body needs all categories working together.
lessons.explained: The two children have different health outcomes because one receives a diverse, balanced diet supplying all nutrient types, while the other — despite eating large quantities — is missing key nutrient classes. This introduces the anchoring mystery: understanding exactly what nutrients are, what they do, and what happens to them inside the body when we eat ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans.

lessons.title: Types of Nutrition & Nutrient Classification
lessons.observed: Students observed images and video clips comparing holozoic feeding (humans eating ugali), parasitic nutrition (tapeworm inside a host's intestine), and saprophytic nutrition (mould growing on stale mandazi). They then examined a nutrient classification chart and matched foods to nutrient classes.
lessons.learned: Animals obtain nutrition in three modes — holozoic (ingesting whole food and digesting it internally), parasitic (absorbing digested nutrients from a living host), and saprophytic (secreting enzymes onto dead matter and absorbing the products). Holozoic nutrition in humans requires six classes of nutrients: carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, vitamins, minerals, and water — each with distinct roles in the body.
lessons.explained: Humans are holozoic feeders, meaning every nutrient must be consumed, digested, and absorbed from the food they eat. The ugali-sukuma wiki-beans meal contains several nutrient classes, but its exact composition — and whether it supplies everything the body needs — depends on understanding what each nutrient class does. This begins to explain why simply eating 'enough' food is insufficient if key classes are missing.

lessons.title: Food Testing Practical: What Is Actually Inside Our Food?
lessons.observed: Students conducted food tests on samples of ugali (maize flour), sukuma wiki (kale), beans, cooking oil, and milk using: iodine solution (starch), Benedict's reagent (reducing sugars), Biuret reagent (proteins), and the ethanol emulsion test (lipids). Results were recorded in a comparison table.
lessons.learned: Different foods contain different classes of nutrients detectable through specific chemical reagents. Ugali tests strongly positive for starch; beans test positive for both starch and protein; sukuma wiki tests positive for reducing sugars and contains vitamins and minerals not detected by these reagents; cooking oil tests positive for lipids. No single food contains all nutrient classes in adequate amounts.
lessons.explained: The food tests provide direct chemical evidence for why the ugali-sukuma wiki-beans combination is nutritionally stronger than ugali alone. Each food in the meal contributes different nutrient classes. However, eating this meal once is not enough — the body must physically and chemically break these nutrients down before they can be used, which raises the next question: how does digestion actually work?

lessons.title: The Digestive System: Structure, Organs & Mechanical Digestion
lessons.observed: Students labelled a diagram of the human alimentary canal and associated organs (salivary glands, liver, pancreas, gall bladder). They observed a demonstration of mechanical digestion — breaking a chapati into smaller pieces by hand to model how teeth and churning increase surface area — and examined the structure of different tooth types.
lessons.learned: The alimentary canal is a continuous tube from mouth to anus, with specialised regions: mouth (ingestion, mechanical digestion), oesophagus (peristalsis), stomach (churning, protein digestion begins), small intestine (main site of chemical digestion and absorption), large intestine (water reabsorption), and rectum/anus (egestion). Mechanical digestion by teeth and stomach muscles increases surface area to speed up chemical digestion.
lessons.explained: When ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans enter the mouth, the first process is mechanical digestion — teeth physically break the food into smaller pieces, increasing surface area for enzymes to act on. This mechanical breakdown is essential preparation for chemical digestion. Without it, the large food molecules would be broken down too slowly. The journey of a meal through the digestive system is now mapped, setting up the next step: enzyme action.

lessons.title: Chemical Digestion & Enzymes: The Body's Molecular Scissors
lessons.observed: Students observed a starch-and-amylase experiment: starch solution was mixed with salivary amylase and tested with iodine at 1-minute intervals, observing the colour change from blue-black to yellow as starch was digested. A control (starch without enzyme) was compared. Students also completed a digestion pathway diagram tracing carbohydrates, proteins, and lipids from ingestion to small molecules.
lessons.learned: Chemical digestion uses specific enzymes to break large, insoluble nutrient molecules into small, soluble, absorbable units. Salivary amylase breaks starch → maltose (mouth); pepsin breaks proteins → polypeptides (stomach, acidic pH); pancreatic amylase, proteases, and lipase act in the small intestine; bile emulsifies fats (increasing surface area for lipase). Final products: glucose, amino acids, fatty acids, and glycerol.
lessons.explained: The starch in ugali is broken down by amylase starting in the mouth and continuing in the small intestine, producing glucose. The protein in beans is broken down by proteases into amino acids. The lipids in any oil used in cooking sukuma wiki are emulsified by bile and digested by lipase into fatty acids and glycerol. This molecular breakdown is what makes nutrients usable — large molecules cannot cross cell membranes, but small molecules can. This answers part of the driving question: it is not enough to eat nutrients; the body must chemically dismantle them first.

lessons.title: Absorption, Assimilation & Egestion: What the Body Does After Digestion
lessons.observed: Students examined a diagram and 3D model of a villus, identifying its key adaptations: thin epithelium (one cell thick), large surface area from finger-like projections and microvilli, rich blood capillary network, and central lacteal. They traced the route of glucose and amino acids into blood capillaries, and fatty acids/glycerol into lacteals. Students also observed a diagram of the large intestine showing water reabsorption.
lessons.learned: Absorption occurs mainly in the small intestine through villi and microvilli. Glucose and amino acids pass into blood capillaries → hepatic portal vein → liver → general circulation. Fatty acids and glycerol pass into lacteals → lymphatic system → bloodstream. Assimilation is the use of absorbed nutrients by body cells: glucose for respiration, amino acids for protein synthesis, lipids for cell membranes and energy storage. Water is reabsorbed in the large intestine; undigested material is egested as faeces.
lessons.explained: Absorption and assimilation complete the journey of the ugali-sukuma wiki-beans meal: the glucose from ugali's starch fuels cellular respiration for energy; the amino acids from beans' protein are used to build and repair body tissues; vitamins and minerals from sukuma wiki support enzyme function and immune health. A child whose diet consistently supplies all these nutrient classes will thrive; a child missing key classes will show deficiency symptoms — connecting directly back to the two children in Lesson 1.

lessons.title: Malnutrition, Deficiency Diseases & Designing a Balanced Diet
lessons.observed: Students analysed case study cards describing children in different Kenyan counties with symptoms of kwashiorkor (protein deficiency), marasmus (energy deficiency), scurvy (Vitamin C deficiency), rickets (Vitamin D/calcium deficiency), and anaemia (iron deficiency). They matched symptoms to missing nutrients and discussed real dietary patterns that cause each condition. Students then designed a one-day balanced meal plan for a 14-year-old Kenyan student using locally available foods.
lessons.learned: Eating sufficient quantities of food does not guarantee good health — the type and variety of food consumed determines whether the body receives all essential nutrients. Protein deficiency causes kwashiorkor (swollen abdomen, discoloured hair); severe energy and protein deficiency causes marasmus (severe wasting); Vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy; Vitamin D/calcium deficiency causes rickets; iron deficiency causes anaemia. A balanced diet must include all six nutrient classes in appropriate proportions, achievable with affordable Kenyan foods.
lessons.explained: The mystery of the two children is now fully explained: the child with poor health outcomes is suffering from malnutrition — not starvation, but nutrient imbalance. Eating large amounts of a single food (e.g., ugali only) supplies carbohydrates but leaves the body deficient in proteins, vitamins, and minerals. The solution is dietary diversity using locally available, affordable foods. A meal combining ugali + beans + sukuma wiki + a small amount of oil covers carbohydrates, protein, lipids, vitamins, and minerals — a practical, low-cost balanced diet.

lessons.title: Synthesis & Model Revision: Solving the Driving Question
lessons.observed: Students revisited the two children case studies from Lesson 1 with all accumulated knowledge. They completed a full revision of their digestive system model — annotating the journey of ugali, sukuma wiki, and beans from ingestion through mechanical digestion, chemical digestion (with enzymes named), absorption (via villi), assimilation (at target tissues), and egestion. Students also reviewed and updated their Driving Question Board, marking questions as answered.
lessons.learned: The complete answer to the driving question integrates all sub-strand concepts: nutrient classification, food testing, digestive system anatomy, mechanical and chemical digestion, enzyme specificity, absorption through villi, assimilation by body cells, and the consequences of malnutrition. Good health requires not just 'enough food' but the right types of food in adequate variety so that all six nutrient classes are supplied and can be properly digested, absorbed, and assimilated.
lessons.explained: Two children eating 'enough food' can have very different health outcomes because food quantity is not the same as nutritional quality. Child A — eating ugali, beans, sukuma wiki, and fruit — receives a balanced supply of all nutrient classes; digestion dismantles these into glucose, amino acids, fatty acids, glycerol, vitamins, and minerals; absorption through villi delivers them to the bloodstream; assimilation uses them to fuel energy, build tissues, and maintain immunity. Child B — eating mainly ugali and little else — is deficient in proteins, vitamins, and minerals despite feeling 'full,' leading to deficiency diseases. The lesson of this sub-strand is that understanding the science of nutrition empowers students to make dietary choices that protect their own and their community's health.
